context: But with many of them, God was not well pleased.
point_summary: Paul's citation of Israel's wilderness failure in 1 Corinthians 10:5 confirms that complaining was the specific conduct that brought divine displeasure, establishing it as a New Testament warning against murmuring for the church.

context: Neither murmur ye, as some of them also murmured and were destroyed of the destroyer.
point_summary: Paul's citation of Israel's murmuring in 1 Corinthians 10:10 confirms that complaining is among the specific behaviors Scripture identifies as displeasing to God, carrying consequences of destruction.

context: But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory.
point_summary: Victory over sin, death, and the grave belongs to Christ, and it is because he is victorious that God gives believers that same victory.

context: For this cause, many are asleep.
point_summary: Failure to respond to God's chastening carries mortal consequences, as Scripture records that some believers have died for this very reason.

context: ...how that his birthright was forfeited...

context: you know the Bible talks about let a man examine himself, right? We read that often, we observe the Lord's Supper and what appropriate passage that is in 1 Corinthians 11.
point_summary: Self-examination commanded in 1 Corinthians 11 for the Lord's Supper extends, the sermon notes, to a daily readiness to give account before God.

context: ...the Bible tells us in 1 Corinthians 2 and verse 14, that the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God...
point_summary: The unregenerate person's rejection of spiritual truth—being unable to receive the things of the Spirit—explains why unbelievers hold a different worldview, and calls believers to respond with compassion rather than treating them as enemies.

context: Paul wrote under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God in 1 Corinthians 13
point_summary: 1 Corinthians 13 is named alongside 1 John 4 as a parallel scriptural pillar on the subject of love, written under the same divine inspiration, without further exposition in this segment.

context: when we understand that we've been bought with a price
point_summary: Bought with a price, believers serve Christ himself—not men—so that unacknowledged effort or withheld appreciation cannot become grounds for growing weary in well-doing.

context: therefore my beloved brethren be
point_summary: Steadfast, unmovable labor for the Lord is sustainable because God's promise guarantees that no faithful work done in His name is ultimately in vain.

context: our labor is not in vain. Help us to remain steadfast and unmovable
point_summary: Steadfastness in well-doing is grounded in the assurance that labor for Christ is not in vain, a promise invoked here as the closing anchor of the sermon's call to perseverance.

reference_text: this mortal shall put on immortality, that this corruption shall put on incorruption

point_summary: The mortal body's future transformation into immortality — "this mortal shall put on immortality" — grounds the assurance, echoed in D.L. Moody's testimony, that the redeemed dead are more alive than ever.

context: The Bible tells us in the book of 1 Corinthians that God, the Holy Spirit, that he gives the gift to every man as he will
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's sovereign distribution of gifts—"to every man as he will"—establishes that God's equipping is individually tailored, making each believer personally responsible for the gifts they have received.

context: For the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God. For there are foolishness unto him, neither can he know them. Why? Because they are spiritually discerned, 1 Corinthians 2.

context: It is Christ who gives the increase.
point_summary: Christ alone gives the church its growth, a corrective Paul applies to the carnal, faction-ridden Corinthians who were wrongly crediting human leaders with the increase.

context: The beginning of chapter 3 deals with that.
point_summary: The carnality and factionalism of the Corinthian church sets the stage for Paul's corrective principle that Christ, not human leaders, is the one who gives the church its growth.

context: Chapter 1 addresses the divisions and all that's taking place there in Corinth.

context: who then is Paul and who is Apollos? But ministers by whom ye believed, even as the Lord gave to every man.

context: I have planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase.

context: We find that 1 Corinthians and 2 Corinthians both deal with this teaching and this truth with regard to the judgment seat of Christ

context: Oh death where is thy sting Oh great where is thy victory

context: The sting of death is sin the strength of sin is the law
point_summary: Death's sting is sin and the law's condemnation, but Christ's resurrection victory—as the conquering, ever-living High Priest—removes that sting for believers.

context: But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Victory over sin, death, and the grave through Christ's resurrection is the ground on which Paul's triumphant declaration—"thanks be to God which giveth us the victory"—rests, as cited here to confirm Christ's conquering, ever-living priesthood.

context: He uses the term in the book of 1 Corinthians that we're a body of the body of Christ, a body that is fitly framed together, a body that is to weep with those that weep, to rejoice with those that rejoice.

point_summary: The natural man's inability to receive spiritual truth (1 Cor. 2:14) explains why distributing Scripture alone is insufficient—someone must guide the reader into understanding, as the eunuch's question illustrates.

context: That's what the scriptures teach us in 1 Corinthians 11.
point_summary: 1 Corinthians 11 is cited as the scriptural basis for understanding the Lord's Supper as a memorial that pictures Christ's death until his return, establishing the pattern for how the church's ordinances function as pictures.

context: You read 1 Corinthians chapter number one. You read how that God has ordained the preaching of His Word.
point_summary: God has ordained preaching as His chosen instrument, and the eunuch's conversion through Philip's proclamation illustrates that divine design in action.

context: Notice in 1 Corinthians chapter number 1, notice the Bible says this in verse number 18, for the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness, but unto us which are saved it is the power of God.
point_summary: The preaching of the cross, though dismissed as foolishness by the perishing, is the very power of God—reason enough to simply give the Bible rather than fear the credentials of false teachers.

context: Verse number 23, we preach Christ crucified unto the Jews, the stumbling block, unto the Greeks foolishness, but unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ, the power of God and the wisdom of God.
point_summary: The simple proclamation of Christ crucified, though deemed foolishness by the world, carries the power and wisdom of God—sufficient reason not to fear intellectually superior false teachers.

context: Look at verse number 26, for you see your calling brethren, how that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble are called, but God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise, and God hath chosen the weak things
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the unimpressive and weak to confound the wise, so believers need not fear false teachers' credentials or sophistication when they have the truth of Scripture.

context: and base things of the world and things which are despised hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not to bring to naught things that are
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the base, despised, and insignificant things of the world to nullify what the world counts as mighty, so that no human being can boast before Him.

context: that no flesh should glory in his presence
point_summary: God's deliberate choice of the world's foolish and weak things—so that no flesh should glory in his presence—establishes that all sufficiency belongs to Christ alone.

context: but of him, in verse number. 30, are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God has made unto us wisdom and righteousness and sanctification and redemption
point_summary: Christ himself, made unto believers wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption, is the ground of all true sufficiency—so that glory belongs to God alone, not to human flesh.

context: that according as it is written, he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord
point_summary: God's design in choosing the weak and base things of the world is that no flesh should glory in His presence, so that all boasting is redirected to the Lord alone.

context: There's a contrast in the second chapter of the book of 1 Corinthians in verses 6 and 7 between worldly wisdom and godly wisdom
point_summary: A sharp contrast exists in 1 Corinthians 2:6 between the wisdom of this world—which comes to nothing—and the hidden wisdom of God ordained before the world.

context: There's a contrast in the second chapter of the book of 1 Corinthians in verses 6 and 7 between worldly wisdom and godly wisdom
point_summary: God's wisdom, described in 1 Corinthians 2:7 as hidden and ordained before the world, stands in direct contrast to the perishing wisdom of this world's rulers.

context: it's here in 1st Corinthians chapter 11 that we really have the most detailed Instruction regarding the Lord's Supper
point_summary: First Corinthians 11 contains the most detailed New Testament instruction on the Lord's Supper, given precisely because the carnal, divided Corinthian church was observing it wrongly and needed correction.

context: Notice what the Bible says 1st Corinthians 11 and verse 23 for I have received of the Lord That which also I delivered unto you
point_summary: Paul's phrase "I have received of the Lord that which I delivered unto you" establishes that the Lord's Supper instructions carry divine, not merely apostolic, authority.

context: For I have received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you, That the Lord Jesus the same night in which he was betrayed took bread:
point_summary: Paul's authority in delivering the Lord's Supper ordinance rests not in his own opinion but in direct reception from the Lord, making the instruction divine rather than apostolic invention.

context: And when he had given thanks, he brake it, and said, Take, eat: this is my body, which is broken for you: this do in remembrance of me.
point_summary: The broken bread of the Lord's Supper is identified as one of two divinely ordained elements, with verse 24 introducing the bread and verse 25 the cup.

context: After the same manner also he took the cup, when he had supped, saying, This cup is the new testament in my blood: this do ye, as oft as ye drink it, in remembrance of me.
point_summary: The cup of the New Testament in Christ's blood, like the bread, carries the repeated command "this do in remembrance of me," identifying both elements as distinct yet unified acts of memorial.

context: For as often as ye eat this bread, and drink this cup, ye do shew the Lord's death till he come.
point_summary: Every observance of the Lord's Supper is a proclamation of Christ's death, continuing until He returns — making the ordinance both a memorial and a forward-looking declaration.

context: Wherefore whosoever shall eat this bread, and drink this cup of the Lord, unworthily, shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord.
point_summary: Partaking of the Lord's Supper unworthily brings guilt of the body and blood of Christ, a warning Paul grounds in the sacred origin of the ordinance he received directly from the Lord.

context: But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of that bread, and drink of that cup.
point_summary: Self-examination is the required prerequisite for partaking of the Lord's Supper, with the command "let a man examine himself" standing as the dividing line between worthy and unworthy participation.

context: For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body.
point_summary: Eating and drinking the Lord's Supper unworthily brings judgment—described here as damnation—upon those who fail to discern the Lord's body.

context: For this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep.

point_summary: The cup of verse 25 is identified as representing the precious blood of Christ, with unfermented grape juice specified as appropriate because leaven and fermentation typify sin, which Christ was without.

context: wherefore Whosoever shall eat this bread and drink this cup of the Lord Unworthily shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord
point_summary: Partaking of the Lord's Supper unworthily incurs guilt of Christ's body and blood, grounding the sermon's argument that communion is restricted to saved, scripturally baptized church members.

context: for as often as ye eat this bread and drink this cup

context: when he had given thanks, he break it and said, take, eat. This is my body which is broken for you, this doing remembrance of me.

context: he took the cup when he had supped, saying, this cup is the New Testament in my blood, this do you as often as you drink it in remembrance of me.
point_summary: The cup of the New Covenant is Christ's blood—a price planned before creation and the only sufficient payment for redemption, which no mere human death could accomplish.

context: for as often as you eat this bread and drink this cup, notice what it says, you do show the Lord's death till he come.

context: The Bible says in verse 28, but let a man examine himself and so let him eat of that bread and drink of that cup.

context: For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body.
point_summary: Eating and drinking at the Lord's Table without discerning the Lord's body brings judgment, making self-examination before communion not optional but a solemn, necessary standard measured against Christ and his Word.

context: He says in verse number 30, for this cause many are weak and sickly among you and many sleep.

context: for if we would judge ourselves we should not be judged

context: let a man examine himself
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Table demands honesty measured against the standard of Christ and his Word, not self-comparison, lest one eat and drink unworthily.

context: Paul answers that question in 1 Corinthians 15, verses three and four.

context: In verse number nine of 1 Corinthians chapter number six, he's writing. to the same group of people that had gone in to Corinth, he had preached the gospel, people got saved, and praised the Lord for it. point_summary: The gospel's converting power is evidenced by the Corinthians themselves, who had been fornicators, idolaters, drunkards, and extortioners, yet were now washed and sanctified.

context: shall not inherit the kingdom of God? Be not deceived, neither fornicators, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor effeminate, nor abusers of themselves with mankind,
point_summary: The Corinthians' transformation from a catalog of vices to the washed, sanctified, and justified illustrates that the gospel converts where the law only condemns.

context: nor thieves, nor covetous, nor drunkards, nor revilers, nor extortioners shall inherit the kingdom of God.

context: And such were some of you, such were some of you, but ye are washed, but ye are sanctified, but ye are justified in the name of the Lord Jesus and by the Spirit of our God.
point_summary: The transformed lives of the Corinthians—once marked by gross sin but now washed, sanctified, and justified—demonstrate that the gospel converts where the law only condemns.

context: The Bible teaches us the book of 1 Corinthians.
point_summary: Every believer has been spiritually gifted and equipped by the Spirit according to His will, for the edification of the church, which Scripture likens to a body where every member matters.

context: For the body is not one member, but many.
point_summary: Every member of the body has a purpose and a place, and no one gift or person can claim greater importance than another within God's design for the church.

context: And the eye cannot say unto the hand, I have no need of thee: nor again the head to the feet, I have no need of you.
point_summary: Every member of the church body has a distinct purpose and place, so no one part can claim greater importance than another, just as Paul argues in the Corinthian body analogy.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number 10 tells us of some Old Testament biblical accounts where the Bible says that with many of them, God was not well-pleased.
point_summary: The negative examples of Old Testament Israelites in 1 Corinthians 10, with whom God was not well-pleased, serve as a warning motivating the desire to learn how to please God.

context: Notice what the Bible says in 1 Corinthians one, in verse number 10, now I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Paul's appeal for unity in the divided Corinthian church is grounded not in his own authority or ambition but explicitly in the name of Christ, and his standard is absolute—no divisions, not merely fewer.

context: I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that ye all speak the same thing, and that there be no divisions among you; but that ye be perfectly joined together in the same mind and in the same judgment.
point_summary: Paul's appeal for "no divisions" in a church already fractured by factions establishes that God's standard for unity is absolute—not reduced divisions, but none—and is grounded in the authority of Christ's name, not apostolic preference.

context: For it hath been declared unto me of you, my brethren, by them which are of the house of Chloe, that there are contentions among you.
point_summary: Divisions and contentions in the Corinthian church serve as the negative backdrop against which Paul's call for complete unity—no divisions, same mind, same judgment—reveals God's standard for the church.

context: Now this I say, that every one of you saith, I am of Paul; and I of Apollos; and I of Cephas; and I of Christ.
point_summary: Partisan allegiances within the Corinthian church—"I am of Paul, I am of Apollos"—illustrate the very schisms Paul's call to unity in verse 10 was written to correct.

context: But I want to preach a message this evening from this thought in 1 Corinthians 15, and I have titled the message, spiritual distancing.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We find in 1 Corinthians 15 and verse number 33, the verse that we'll use as our study this evening, the Bible says this, be not deceived.
point_summary: Paul's warning that "evil communications corrupt good manners" is applied here as a call to "spiritual distancing"—deliberately guarding one's relationships and associations against wicked or ungodly influences that corrupt character.

context: be not deceived, evil communications corrupt good manners
point_summary: Paul's warning that evil companionships corrupt good character calls believers to practice deliberate "spiritual distancing" from wicked or ungodly relationships, guarding against the deception that such associations are harmless.

context: be not deceived, evil communications corrupt good manners
point_summary: Paul's warning that "evil communications corrupt good manners" is expounded as a caution against ungodly companionships that gradually deprave virtuous character, with the repeated phrase "be not deceived" underscoring how easily believers underestimate that corrupting influence.

context: this verse of Scripture from 1 Corinthians 15 where they did not take heed to the warning that God had given them
point_summary: Paul's warning against self-deception in 1 Corinthians 15:33 is treated as a timeless principle illustrated by Israel's history: when God's people dismiss the corrupting influence of ungodly associations, the culture shapes the church rather than the church shaping the culture.

context: Evil communications corrupt good manners. And we find that they're mingling, they're fellowshipping with the heathen and they're learning their works and they're serving their idols.
point_summary: Mingling with ungodly companions leads not to a single dramatic fall but down a gradual, corrupting slope—from fellowship to imitation to idolatry—illustrating that evil communications corrupt good manners.

context: when we think about 1 Corinthians 15, 33, we think of it in the context of children or maybe even more specifically, teenagers. But you understand that evil communications corrupt good manners in adults as well, in the workplace, in the neighborhood, right? Those evil communications, they corrupt good manners.
point_summary: The warning "be not deceived" in 1 Corinthians 15:33 applies universally across all ages and life settings, not merely to youth, because evil communications corrupt good manners in adults just as surely.

context: I mentioned last Wednesday evening. I think I said it was 1 Corinthians 11, it's 1 Corinthians 10 and verse number 12 where the Bible says, let him that thinketh he stand take heed lest he fall.
point_summary: Self-confidence in the face of corrupting influences is itself a form of deception, and the warning "let him that thinketh he stand take heed lest he fall" checks the assumption that one can control sinful circumstances.

context: no doubt the truth that we're looking at in 1 Corinthians 15, 33 is seen in these verses of scripture that evil communications corrupt good manners.
point_summary: Evil communications corrupt good manners is a universal biblical principle—not merely a warning for youth—that applies equally to adults in every sphere of life, and demands deliberate spiritual distancing from corrupting influences.

context: be not deceived evil communications corrupt good manners
point_summary: The warning of 1 Corinthians 15:33 — that evil communications corrupt good manners — grounds a call to "spiritual distancing," illustrated by Israel's failure to separate from Canaan's inhabitants and reinforced by 2 Corinthians 6's command to come out and be separate.

context: be not deceived, evil communications corrupt good manners
point_summary: Paul's warning that evil company corrupts good character is presented as a call to spiritual separation, illustrated by Psalm 1's progressive descent from walking to standing to sitting among the ungodly.

context: Be not deceived. Evil communications corrupt good manners.
point_summary: The antidote to deception is immersion in God's Word, because knowing truth equips believers to recognize and resist the corrupting influence of ungodly fellowship warned against in this verse.

context: in 1 Corinthians 15, 33. And so it's really a just kind of smacking in the face on this subject of deception. And so be not deceived.
point_summary: Paul's double use of "be not deceived" in the Greek frames the verse as an urgent, emphatic warning that ungodly associations will corrupt virtuous character, demanding deliberate "spiritual distancing."

context: But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Victory over the world belongs to believers not by their own strength but through the Lord Jesus Christ, whose resurrection triumph is the ground of the Christian's confident, undefeated standing.

context: And thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory.
point_summary: Victory over the world belongs to Christ, and believers share in it — grounding the command to "be of good cheer" in the triumph of the one who has overcome.

context: ...use an illustration that is quite similar to the one used in 1 Corinthians nine...
point_summary: Paul's athletic illustration in 2 Timothy 2:5 is drawn from the same competitive-games imagery he developed in 1 Corinthians 9, connecting disciplined striving to the life of a good soldier.

context: Notice in 1 Corinthians 9, and then we'll come back to 2 Timothy, because we're gonna see in 2 Timothy later on how this is illustrated, and we'll deal with that. But turn over just briefly to 1 Corinthians 9, if you will.
point_summary: Paul's athletic metaphor in 1 Corinthians 9 is cited to show that the disciplined soldier, like the disciplined runner, strives purposefully to obtain the prize rather than cutting corners or taking the easy way.

context: And he says in verse 23, and this I do for the gospel's sake that I might be partaker thereof with you.
point_summary: Paul's declaration in 1 Corinthians 9:23 — doing all things for the gospel's sake — introduces his athletic imagery of disciplined, purposeful striving, contrasted with cutting corners, toward an incorruptible crown rather than a perishable one.

context: Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize. So what does he tell them? So run that ye may obtain.
point_summary: Competing athletes submit to rigorous discipline to win a perishable crown, making them a rebuke to believers who cut corners rather than pursue the incorruptible prize with equal self-denial.

context: Notice in verse 25, and every man that striveth for the mastery, remember we saw in 2 Timothy 2 five that they that strive for masteries, we see it here in verse 25, and every man that striveth for the mastery, notice what it says, is temperate in all things.
point_summary: Striving athletes submit to total self-discipline for a perishable crown, making their temperance a rebuke to any soldier of Christ who seeks shortcuts while competing for an incorruptible one.

context: Those in the Ishmaelian games, they would stand before the judges, if you will, and as they would stand there, in fact, even call a behemoth seat. point_summary: Whereas athletes at the Isthmian Games competed for a corruptible crown, 1 Corinthians 9 contrasts that with the incorruptible crown awaiting the disciplined believer, motivating a soldier's life of godly self-mastery.

context: I want you to look over at 1 Corinthians chapter number 2
point_summary: Only those who have received the Spirit of God can understand or embrace biblical truth, which is why expecting unbelievers to live and act biblically is unreasonable.

context: But notice the truth of 1 Corinthians 2 and verse 14. The Bible says that the natural man, do you understand what God means when He says the natural man? context: and then we'll transition into 1 Corinthians 11 and observe the Lord's Supper this evening.

point_summary: Paul's use of Israel's wilderness failures in 1 Corinthians 10 illustrates that Scripture plainly identifies behaviors that displease God, so believers can know what to avoid.

context: I know that I am what I am by the grace of God.
point_summary: Paul's confession "I am what I am by the grace of God" is invoked to reinforce the psalmist's declaration of poverty and neediness, grounding both in total dependence on divine grace.

context: And in first Corinthians one and verse 22, he says that the Jews require a sign.
point_summary: Paul's observation that Jews require a sign traces back to Abram himself, who asked God for a confirming sign regarding the inheritance of the land.

context: But let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of that bread and drink of that cup.
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Supper—letting a man search his own "tent" for hidden sin before eating and drinking—is the prescribed guard against partaking unworthily.

context: For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body.
point_summary: Eating and drinking the Lord's Supper without self-examination brings judgment, making honest confession before the table not optional but a matter of spiritual health and integrity.

context: For this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep.
point_summary: Unconfessed sin carried to the Lord's Table brings divine judgment, making self-examination before Communion a solemn necessity rather than a mere formality.

context: Oh, death, where is thy sting?
point_summary: Death's sting is removed for the believer because Christ has conquered death, grounding the heavenly citizenship and fearless perspective Abraham's sojourner confession illustrates.

context: The Bible tells us in the book of 1 Corinthians that the natural man receiveth not the things of the spirit of God. Why? Because they are foolishness unto him. They must be spiritually discerned.
point_summary: The natural man's inability to receive spiritual truth (1 Cor. 2:14) explains why skeptics approach Scripture with faulty presuppositions, while believers must come to God's Word submissively, allowing it to correct their thinking.

context: ...I want to remind you of 1 Corinthians chapter 11. We have our Lord's Supper table before us...
point_summary: God's judgment is not absent from the New Testament: unworthy participation in the Lord's Supper brought weakness, sickness, and even death upon members of the Corinthian church.

context: Those that would eat and drink unworthily, it says, for this cause many are weak and sickly among you, and many sleep.
point_summary: God's judgment remains active in the New Testament era, as seen in 1 Corinthians 11:30, where unworthy participation in the Lord's Supper brought weakness, sickness, and death upon believers.

context: and for this cause, many are sick and weakly among you, and many sleep.
point_summary: Unrepentant believers who resist conviction and chastisement face divine judgment up to premature death, as Paul's warning that "many sleep" illustrates God's active discipline within the New Testament church.

reference_text: whether therefore you eat or drink or whatsoever you do, do all to the glory of God
context: but the reality is that we are saved by the grace of God and everything about us is to be lived for the glory and honor of the Lord. Whether therefore you eat or drink or whatsoever you do, do all to the glory of God.
point_summary: Every area of life — eating, drinking, all activity — falls under the call to glorify God, with no valid distinction between sacred and secular categories.

context: And I, brethren, could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal, even as unto babes in Christ.
point_summary: Carnality, not the teacher's inadequacy, is what prevents a congregation from receiving deeper biblical truth, as Paul's inability to speak to the Corinthians "as unto spiritual" parallels the Hebrews passage's rebuke of dullness.

context: I have fed you with milk and not with meat. For hitherto you were not able to bear it,
point_summary: Carnality, not complexity, was the barrier to deeper teaching — Paul withheld solid doctrinal meat from the Corinthians not from ignorance but because their spiritual immaturity left them unable to receive it.

context: For ye are yet carnal, for whereas there is among you envying and strife and divisions, are ye not carnal and walk as men?
point_summary: Carnality, not ignorance, was the barrier to Paul's deeper teaching in Corinth — the Corinthians' envying, strife, and divisions marked them as spiritually immature and unready to receive meatier instruction.

context: different from what we find in 1 Corinthians three
point_summary: Carnality — having one foot in the world and one foot in the church — is identified in 1 Corinthians 3 as the traceable cause of stunted spiritual growth.

context: but we preach Christ crucified unto the Jews a stumbling block and unto the Greeks foolishness.
point_summary: The message of Christ crucified—dismissed as foolishness and weakness by the world—is cited to show that God deliberately works through weakness so that no human flesh receives the glory.

context: But unto them which are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God.
point_summary: Christ being called "the power of God and the wisdom of God" for the called establishes that divine strength perfected in weakness is not an anomaly but the consistent pattern of how God works.

context: because the foolishness of God is wiser than men. The weakness of God is stronger than men.
point_summary: God deliberately works through what the world counts as weak and foolish so that no human being can claim credit for the victory.

context: For ye see your calling, brethren, how that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble, are called.
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the weak, foolish, and base things of the world so that no human flesh can claim glory for what He accomplishes.

context: But God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise.
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the weak, foolish, and base things of the world so that no human flesh can claim credit for the victory He accomplishes.

context: And base things of the world and things which are despised hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not to bring to naught things that are
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the base, despised, and weak things of the world so that no human flesh can claim glory in His presence.

context: that no flesh should glory in His presence
point_summary: God's deliberate choice of the weak, base, and despised things of the world serves the singular purpose that no human flesh should boast before Him.

context: I am what I am by the grace of God
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible tells us in 1 Corinthians 10, let him that thinketh he standeth, take heed lest he fall.
point_summary: The warning that even believers can sink to deep levels of carnality grounds the call to walk in the Spirit rather than assume personal immunity to fleshly failure.

context: The Spirit, the Holy Spirit of God, giveth to every man severally as he will, according to 1 Corinthians chapter 12. We find that in verse number seven of 1 Corinthians 12, it says that it's given to every man.
point_summary: Every spiritual gift is given by God himself, not earned by the recipient, so no believer has grounds for pride in whatever gift he possesses.

context: Paul had a certain position, but even think about what he said to the believers in Corinth in 1 Corinthians chapter 3, in the midst of all of the divisions and all of the carnality and all that was taking place, and there's a group of people saying, I'm of Paul, and I'm of Apollos, and I'm of Cephas
point_summary: Paul's reminder in 1 Corinthians 3 that ministers are merely co-laborers with God—not independent agents—grounds the call to humility by stripping away any basis for pride in spiritual accomplishment.

context: God uses some of us to plant, God uses some of us to water, but God's the one that gives the increase, and God's, listen, God allows us, I love the way that he says it in 1 Corinthians 3, in verse number 9, we are laborers together, it doesn't say for God, it says with God.
point_summary: Being called "laborers together with God" rather than merely laborers for God underscores that ministry is a God-granted privilege, not a ground for human boasting or factional pride.

context: Paul says this in 1 Corinthians chapter 15. Again, a verse of scripture that we regularly hear or refer to, and Paul says, the beginning of the verse, but by the grace of God, I am what I am. And his grace, it's not my grace, it's his grace, he's the source of grace, which was bestowed upon me was not in vain, but I labored more abundantly
point_summary: Grace, not personal merit, is both the source of what Paul is and the power behind all his labor — a perspective that guards against pride while affirming God-given identity and calling.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number nine, if you have your Bibles, turn there to that passage of scripture in 1 Corinthians chapter number nine. We're looking from verses 16 down through 27.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Here in 1 Corinthians 9, the apostle Paul is really sharing his testimony and sharing his burden, if you will, for preaching the gospel.
point_summary: Paul's absolute surrender to God's will is presented as the foundational reason he was used so greatly, framing 1 Corinthians 9 as his personal testimony of gospel burden born from that surrender.

context: We find here in 1 Corinthians 9, the apostle Paul, I'm gonna read a verse and then we'll get into the text
point_summary: Paul's declaration in verse 23 — "this I do for the gospel's sake" — is presented as the defining motive of his ministry, rooted in a life of absolute surrender to God's will.

context: For though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of. For necessity is laid upon me, yea, woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that "necessity is laid upon me" frames gospel preaching not as a voluntary achievement to boast in, but as a divine compulsion and serious responsibility entrusted to him.

context: For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward. But if against my will a dispensation of the gospel is committed unto me.
point_summary: Paul's stewardship of the gospel is not self-chosen glory but a divine commission laid upon him as a solemn responsibility he was bound to take seriously.

context: What is my reward then? Verily that when I preach the gospel, I may. to make the Gospel of Christ without charge, that I abuse not my power in the Gospel.
point_summary: Preaching the gospel without charge was Paul's chosen reward—the mark of a man whose supreme priority was pointing people to Jesus, not personal glory.

context: for though I be free from all men, yet have I made myself servant unto all that I might gain the more.
point_summary: Paul's voluntary self-subordination to all people—free man becoming servant—is not compromise but strategic adaptation in approach so that more might be gained for the gospel.

context: And unto the Jews I became as a Jew, that I might gain the Jews. To them that are under the law is under the law, that I might gain them that are under the law.
point_summary: Paul's contextual adaptation to Jews and Gentiles alike is not compromise but a deliberate strategy to remove relational barriers so that Christ, not cultural offense, remains the issue.

context: To them that are without law is without law, being not without law to God, but under the law to Christ, that I might gain them that are without the law.
point_summary: Paul's adaptability to those "without law" signals not doctrinal compromise but strategic flexibility aimed at gaining a hearing for Christ among Gentile unbelievers.

context: To the weak became I as weak, that I might gain the weak.
point_summary: Becoming "all things to all men" means adapting one's approach to different audiences not as compromise but as strategic flexibility aimed at winning people to Christ.

context: here in 1 Corinthians 9, in verses 19 through 22, I believe that what Paul is seeking to communicate to these believers in Corinth is a philosophy of understanding and not missing the truth of seeing people converted
point_summary: Paul's freedom from all men in 1 Corinthians 9:19–22 is framed as a philosophy of prioritizing genuine conversion over church membership, denominational identity, or any external religious requirement.

context: here in 1 Corinthians 9, in verses 19 through 22, I believe that what Paul is seeking to communicate to these believers in Corinth is a philosophy of understanding and not missing the truth of seeing people converted
point_summary: Paul's flexibility toward all people in 1 Corinthians 9:19–22 is framed as a philosophy of keeping the focus on conversion to Christ rather than church membership or religious affiliation.

context: ...know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize. So run, that ye may obtain...
point_summary: Paul's athletic metaphor from the Isthmian Games frames the Christian life as a purposeful, disciplined race run not for a perishable crown but for an incorruptible one.

context: ...And every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things. Now they do it to obtain a corruptible crown, but we, an incorruptible...
point_summary: Disciplined self-denial for a perishable prize illustrates how much more the believer should strive, since the crown awaiting the faithful is incorruptible rather than corruptible.

context: And every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things. Now they do it to obtain a corruptible crown; but we an incorruptible.
point_summary: Paul's contrast between the athlete's perishable pine wreath and the believer's incorruptible crown establishes that gospel-driven self-discipline is motivated by an eternal prize, not a temporary one.

context: I therefore so run, not as uncertainly; so fight I, not as one that beateth the air:
point_summary: Paul's purposeful, disciplined running—contrasted with aimless effort—reflects his commitment to gospel ministry over the corruptible prizes that motivate even the most rigorous earthly athletes.

context: But I keep under my body, and bring it into subjection: lest that by any means, when I have preached to others, I myself should be a castaway.
point_summary: Paul's self-discipline—keeping his body under subjection—is driven by the fear of becoming a castaway, meaning rejected or rendered useless, after having preached to others.

context: Some water, or some plant, some water, but God giveth the increase.
point_summary: Faithful gospel witness often yields unseen fruit, as Paul's words that one plants and another waters while God gives the increase remind believers their impact may extend far beyond what they can observe.

context: the Bible says who then is Paul and who is Apollos but ministers by whom you
point_summary: God calls His servants not as self-sufficient agents but as ministers—instruments through whom He works, making the witness's identity irrelevant apart from what the Lord gives each one to do.

context: watereth but God that giveth the increase
point_summary: Human effort in evangelism is zero apart from God, who alone gives the increase, making believers nothing more than fellow laborers whose only boast is in Him.

context: now he that planteth and he that watereth are one and every man shall receive his own reward according to his own labor
point_summary: Every believer is a co-laborer with God in gospel work, responsible to plant or water faithfully while crediting God alone with the increase.

context: for we are laborers together with God you're God's husbandry you're God's building
point_summary: Believers are described as "laborers together with God," a partnership that frames gospel work—like carrying a paralyzed man to Jesus—as cooperative effort requiring each person to take their corner.

context: And we're laborers together with God, according to 1 Corinthians 3 and verse number 9.
point_summary: Believers are not solo workers but collaborative partners with God, each accountable for his own labor in bringing others to Christ.

context: He said it this way, I die daily.
point_summary: Paul's phrase "I die daily" is invoked to show that unbroken self-denial is the necessary condition for following Jesus without inner division.

context: Notice the Bible says now in this that I declare unto you, I praise you not, that you come together not for the better, but for the worse.
point_summary: Paul's rebuke — "I praise you not" — frames the entire passage as a correction of assembly conduct that had deteriorated rather than edified, a phrase the sermon notes is repeated in both verse 17 and verse 22.

context: For first of all, when ye come together in the church, I hear that there be divisions among you; and I partly believe it.
point_summary: Divisions reported among the Corinthian church at their gatherings serve as the opening indictment Paul levels before correcting their abuse of the Lord's Supper, consistent with his broader charge of carnality throughout the letter.

context: When ye come together therefore into one place, this is not to eat the Lord's supper.
point_summary: When the Corinthians gathered, their divisions and carnality meant the assembly produced spiritual harm rather than benefit, disqualifying what they called the Lord's Supper from being that at all.

context: For in eating every one taketh before other his own supper: and one is hungry, and another is drunken.
point_summary: The abuse of the Lord's Supper at Corinth had reached the point where some members gorged and got drunk while others went hungry, exposing the church's deep carnality and division.

context: What? have ye not houses to eat and to drink in? or despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not? What shall I say to you? shall I praise you in this? I praise you not.
point_summary: Paul's repeated phrase "I praise you not" in verses 17 and 22 frames his Lord's Supper instruction within a broader rebuke of a carnal, divided church unworthy of commendation.

context: And when he had given thanks, he break it and said, Take, eat. This is my body which is broken for you. This do in remembrance of me.
point_summary: The elements of the Lord's Supper are representative memorials—not saving sacraments or transforming substances—pointing believers to Christ's broken body and shed blood.

context: wherefore, whosoever shall eat this bread and drink this cup of the Lord unworthily shall be guilty of the body and blood of the Lord
point_summary: Partaking of the Lord's Supper "unworthily" is the sermon's central warning, with the text establishing that such participation makes one guilty of the body and blood of Christ.

context: but let a man examine himself and so let him eat of that bread and drink of that cup
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Supper is framed as the sermon's central command, with "unworthily" in the surrounding verses identified as the key word demanding personal scrutiny.

context: for he that eateth and drinketh unworthily, eateth and drinketh damnation to himself, not discerning the Lord's body
point_summary: Eating or drinking the Lord's Supper "unworthily" — a word appearing in both verse 27 and verse 29 — is the sermon's focal concern, with the preacher stressing that no one possesses inherent worthiness before God.

context: For this cause, many are weak and sickly among you and many sleep
point_summary: Physical weakness and death among Corinthian believers are presented as divine discipline for partaking of the Lord's Supper unworthily, without discerning the Lord's body.

context: for if we would judge ourselves, we should not be judged
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Supper is framed as the corrective that spares believers from divine judgment, since judging ourselves removes the need for God to judge us.

context: ...being in a place of verse 27 and verse 29 of being unworthy to partake...
point_summary: Eating and drinking "unworthily" in verse 27 is tied specifically to unconfessed sin, which disqualifies a believer not permanently but until that sin is brought to confession and forgiven.

context: ...being in a place of verse 27 and verse 29 of being unworthy to partake...
point_summary: Eating and drinking "unworthily" in verse 29 is tied specifically to unconfessed sin, with self-examination serving not as grounds for abstention but as the Spirit's prompt to confess and receive forgiveness.

context: flee fornication. Every sin that a man doeth is without the body, but he that committed fornication sinneth against his own body.
point_summary: Sexual sin is uniquely serious because it is committed against the believer's own body, which belongs to God and is the temple of the Holy Spirit — making such sin worse for the Christian than for the lost.

context: What? Know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which you have of God and you're not your own?
point_summary: Because the believer's body is the temple of the Holy Ghost and has been bought with a price, sin in a Christian's life carries a graver weight than sin in an unbeliever's.

context: For you're bought with a price, therefore glorify God in your body and in your spirit which are God's.
point_summary: Because believers are bought with a price and their bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit, sin in a Christian's life carries greater weight than sin in an unbeliever's—making self-examination before the Lord's Supper all the more urgent.

context: but with many of them God was not well pleased, for they were overthrown in the wilderness.
point_summary: God's judgment on Israel in the wilderness—cited as a pattern of divine displeasure with sin—grounds Paul's broader warning that believers must not presume upon grace but examine their conduct before God.

context: Notice in verse 6, now these things were our examples to the intent we should not lust after evil things as they also lusted.
point_summary: Israel's wilderness failures are recorded as cautionary examples for believers, warning that lusting after evil things carries consequences that God's people in any era must heed.

context: Neither be idolaters as were some of them. As it is written, the people sat down to eat and drink and rose up to play.
point_summary: Idolatry in verse 7 is not limited to physical images but includes anything placed before God, warning believers against too quickly dismissing the charge.

context: Neither let us commit fornication as some of them committed and fell in one day three and twenty thousand.
point_summary: Sexual sin is treated as a serious covenant warning drawn from Israel's wilderness failure, cited alongside idolatry and tempting Christ as examples believers must not dismiss too quickly when examining themselves.

context: Neither let us tempt Christ as some of them also tempted and were destroyed of serpents.
point_summary: Testing oneself against the Old Testament warning of 1 Corinthians 10:9 — that Israel tempted Christ and was destroyed by serpents — is presented as one of several self-examination checkpoints a believer must not too quickly dismiss.

context: Neither murmur he as some of them also murmured and were destroyed of the destroyer.
point_summary: Murmuring—easily overlooked beside graver sins—is placed by Paul in the same list of wilderness failures that brought divine destruction, warning believers not to dismiss it as trivial.

context: Now in verse number 11, it says, all these things happened unto them for in samples, and they are written for our admonition upon whom the ends of the world are come.
point_summary: Israel's wilderness failures are recorded as admonitory examples specifically for believers living in the present age, warning that complaining against God's provision displeases Him and that no one is beyond the danger of falling.

context: Wherefore let him that thinketh, he standeth, take heed, lest he fall.
point_summary: Self-confidence that minimizes personal sin invites the warning of verse 12: those who think they stand must take heed, lest they fall, as Israel's failures demonstrate.

context: Verse 13, there hath no temptation taken you, but such as is common to man. But God is faithful, who will not suffer you to be tempted above that you are able, but will with the temptation also make a way to escape, that you may be able to bear it.
point_summary: God's faithfulness in providing a way of escape from temptation is illustrated by contrasting Joseph, who fled Potiphar's wife, with David, implying that the escape route must be actively sought.

reference_text: thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ
context: thanks be to god which giveth us the victory through our lord jesus christ
point_summary: Victory over the enemy is grounded not in human effort but in God's gift, as Paul's doxology — "thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ" — is cited to anchor the sermon's call to spiritual warfare.

context: but Paul says in 1st Corinthians 2 in verse number 14 the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God for their foolishness unto him neither can he know them because they are spiritually discerned
point_summary: Those who reject the Trinity do so not from intellectual failure but from spiritual incapacity, since the natural man cannot receive the things of the Spirit of God.

context: This mortal shall put on immortality, 1 Corinthians 15.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection victory over death transforms the believer's relationship to mortality, so that this corruptible body putting on immortality (1 Cor. 15) becomes the ground for fearless living and confident witness.

context: And I'm reminded of some New Testament verses of Scripture in 1 Corinthians 15, in verse number 58,
point_summary: Steadfast, unfainting service to Christ is not futile—the Ephesian church's faithful labor illustrates Paul's promise that work done in the Lord is never in vain.

context: Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, for as much as you know that your labor is not in vain in the Lord.
point_summary: Faithful, unfainting service for Christ is genuine labor, yet that labor is never in vain—a truth grounding the Ephesian church's commended endurance in Revelation 2:3.

context: As I read verse two, I'm reminded of what we read in 1 Corinthians 15, and in a moment, until we can live an eye, and we read how that this mortal shall put on immortality and this corruption shall be put on incorruption, that we shall be changed.
point_summary: The resurrection truth of 1 Corinthians 15—that the mortal shall put on immortality and the corruptible incorruption—grounds the promise that believers shall be changed and made like Christ at His appearing.

context: behold, I show you a mystery. We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump. For the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, and we shall be changed.
point_summary: The sudden, instantaneous nature of Christ's return — "in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye" — is cited to underscore the certainty and urgency that should shape believers' present behavior.

context: Paul says to the believers in 1 Corinthians chapter number 1, in verse number 9, he says it this way very simply, God is faithful.
point_summary: God's faithfulness, demonstrated in answering Abraham's prayer and healing Abimelech's household, is grounded in the unchanging character Paul simply declares in 1 Corinthians 1:9: "God is faithful."

context: Paul said that there was an open door. Great and effectual. But there were many adversaries.
point_summary: Open doors for effective gospel work, as Paul found, come paired with many adversaries — opposition is the expected companion of faithful advance, not a sign that the work is wrong.

context: But he says in verse 23, but we preach Christ crucified unto the Jews a stumbling block and unto the Greeks foolishness.
point_summary: The cross, though a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Greeks, is the very message Paul preached and the ground of the believer's confidence against the accuser.

context: But, verse 24, unto them which are called both Jews and Greeks, Christ, the power of God, and the wisdom of God.
point_summary: To those who are called—both Jews and Greeks—the cross that is a stumbling block or foolishness is received as Christ the power and wisdom of God.

context: The Bible says in 1 Corinthians 4 and verse number 2, moreover, it's required in Stuart's that a man be found faithful.
point_summary: Faithfulness required of stewards (1 Cor. 4:2) is the standard against which the shepherds' diligent night-watch is measured, illustrating that God expects his people to be found faithful in their appointed duties.

context: In 1 Corinthians 15, in verse number 33, it says this, be not deceived. Now anytime a verse starts with those words, you ought to pay close attention. Why? Because the enemy is a liar. He is a deceiver. Amen? And so here, God says, be not deceived. What is it? Evil communications corrupt good manners.
point_summary: Bad company corrupts good character — a biblical truth, not merely folk wisdom — grounding the command to withdraw from the disorderly in the reality that sinful influence spreads through association.

context: In 1 Corinthians 5, in verse number 6, the Bible says a little leaven, leaveneth the whole lump.
point_summary: A little leaven leavening the whole lump illustrates that disorderly behavior, left unchecked, spreads its corrupting influence throughout the entire church body.

context: no, evil communications corrupt good manners. It is a biblical truth.
point_summary: Evil communications corrupt good manners is presented as a divine warning against self-deception about relational influence, applicable at every stage of life, not only in childhood.

context: We preach Christ, amen, 1 Corinthians
point_summary: The centrality of preaching Christ crucified grounds the broader ministry pattern of warning and teaching that Paul applies to correcting disorderly behavior in the church.

context: notice over the book of 1 Corinthians chapter 15, and I want you to note verse number 33
point_summary: Paired with Hebrews 3's warning about sin's deceitfulness, 1 Corinthians 15:33's command "be not deceived" introduces the principle that evil associations corrupt character—a deception Lot's life illustrates.

context: In 1 Corinthians 15 and verse number 33, notice what the Bible says, be not deceived. That's interesting, is it not? We just talked about the deceitfulness of sin, and now we see that the Word of God says, be not deceived. So, therefore, we understand that what God is preparing to tell us here is given the introductory thought of be not deceived. Why? Because we can be deceived with this thought. And therefore, be not deceived, notice what he says, evil communications corrupt good manners.
point_summary: Sin's power to deceive includes the false confidence that one can associate with evil companions without being corrupted by them, which Scripture directly warns against: evil communications corrupt good manners.

reference_text: Be not deceived, evil communications corrupt good manners.
context: Be not deceived, evil communications corrupt good manners. There's more than one person that has lived and walked upon the face of this earth that thought that they could put themselves into a group of people that were evil people, that were wicked people, ungodly people, and that they would be able to influence them to be good.
point_summary: The self-deceiving belief that one can enter wicked company as a reforming influence is refuted by the plain warning that evil communications corrupt good manners, as Lot's downward spiral illustrates.

context: First Corinthians chapter number one, as you're turning there, I would say this evening, there is little doubt in my mind that you have experienced conflict in your life.
point_summary: Conflict within relationships is not a modern problem; 1 Corinthians 1 shows it is an ancient one the church has always had to address.

context: Now I beseech you, brethren, by the name of our Lord Jesus Christ, that you all speak the same thing and that there be no divisions among you, but that you be perfectly joined together in the same mind and in the same judgment.
point_summary: Paul's appeal for unity — that believers speak the same thing, with no divisions, perfectly joined in mind and judgment — is presented as the direct remedy for the conflict-ridden, people-filled church at Corinth.

context: 'For it hath been declared unto me of you, my brethren, 'by them which are of the house of Chloe, 'that there are contentions among you.'
point_summary: Verse 11 identifies the specific human channel—the house of Chloe—through which Paul was made aware of the contentions at Corinth, prompting his apostolic response.

context: 'Now this I say that every one of you sayeth, 'I am of Paul, and I have Apollos, 'and I have Cephas, and I have Christ.
point_summary: Partisan allegiance to individual leaders — Paul, Apollos, Cephas, or Christ — had fractured the Corinthian church into competing factions, exposing the spiritual immaturity and carnality driving their divisions.

context: 'Is Christ divided? 'Was Paul crucified for you? 'Or were you baptized in the name of Paul?'
point_summary: Paul's rhetorical questions in verse 13 expose the absurdity of personality-based factions by pointing to Christ alone as the one crucified and the only name in which baptism is given.

context: we find this evening that as we look at this thought in 1 Corinthians 1 and verses 10 and 11
point_summary: Unity among believers is both commanded and achievable, with Paul's direct confrontation of division in 1 Corinthians 1:10–11 demonstrating that conflict must be addressed and resolved to honor God.

context: we find this evening that as we look at this thought in 1 Corinthians 1 and verses 10 and 11
point_summary: Paul's awareness of the Corinthian divisions—reported to him by others—illustrates how Satan exploits unresolved conflict within the church to create contention among believers who share a common enemy.

context: The Bible tells us in 1 Corinthians when we read about Paul in chapter number 10, he's referencing several Old Testament events and really in rapid succession.
point_summary: Paul's catalog of Israel's wilderness failures in 1 Corinthians 10 is invoked to underscore that God's displeasure is a real and sobering outcome when His people ignore the warning signs of chastisement.

context: Paul says this, I'll close with this portion of Scripture in 1 Corinthians 11. We often use the portion of Scripture with dealing with the Lord's Supper. context: What? No ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which you have of God. You're not your own. You're bought with a price.

context: Christ, our Passover.
point_summary: The repeated animal sacrifices of the Old Testament could never permanently remove sin but pointed forward to Christ, the true Passover, who would accomplish what they could only foreshadow.

context: Notice what the Bible says in 1 Corinthians chapter 15
point_summary: Christ's bodily resurrection is the very substance of the gospel Paul declares in 1 Corinthians 15, the chapter's opening verses grounding salvation itself in that proclaimed and received truth.

context: Paul says this in verse number one, moreover brethren, I declare unto you the gospel, which I preached unto you, which also you have received, and wherein ye stand
point_summary: The resurrection of Christ is the very content of the gospel Paul declared, received, and passed on — a gospel in which believers stand and by which they are saved.

context: by which also ye are saved, if ye keep in memory what I preached unto you, unless ye have believed in vain.
point_summary: Salvation itself rests on the bodily resurrection of Christ, without which both preaching and faith collapse into vanity, as Paul's own summary of the gospel in this chapter declares.

context: For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures
point_summary: Christ's death for sins, burial, and bodily resurrection on the third day constitute the gospel Paul received and delivered—a historically verified reality confirmed by hundreds of eyewitnesses.

context: and that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the Scriptures
point_summary: Christ's bodily resurrection on the third day, confirmed by multiple eyewitnesses, is presented here as the factual core of the gospel without which all preaching and faith collapse into futility.

context: that he was seen of Cephas, and of the twelve.
point_summary: The post-resurrection appearances of Christ — to Cephas, the Twelve, five hundred witnesses, and Paul himself — are cited as cumulative historical testimony confirming that the resurrection is not myth but verified fact on which the gospel stands.

context: After that he was seen of about five hundred brethren at once, of whom the greater part remain unto this present, but some are fallen asleep.
point_summary: The post-resurrection appearance of Christ to more than five hundred witnesses at once is cited as historical confirmation that the resurrection is not myth but verifiable fact, validating the gospel Paul declares.

context: After that he was seen of James, then of all the apostles, and last of all he was seen of me also, as of one born out of due time.
point_summary: Christ's post-resurrection appearance to James and all the apostles, cited as part of the eyewitness testimony in 1 Corinthians 15 establishing the historical reality of the resurrection.

context: And Paul will go on to say that if Christ be not risen from the dead, then our preaching is in vain, our faith is in vain,
point_summary: Paul's declaration that faithless preaching and vain faith result if Christ is not risen anchors the resurrection as the indispensable foundation of the gospel and the believer's hope.

context: but he declares in verse number 20, but now is Christ risen from the dead and become the firstfruits of them that slept.

context: remain steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord. Why? For we know that our labor is not in vain in the Lord.
point_summary: The call to steadfastness amid perilous times is grounded in the assurance that faithful labor in the Lord is never in vain, making perseverance worthwhile.

context: Know ye not that what? Your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost, which is in you what you have of God, and ye are not your own
point_summary: Because the body belongs to God as His purchased possession and the indwelling Spirit's temple, believers have no autonomous authority over themselves but are obligated to glorify God in every aspect of their being.

context: for you are bought with a price. Therefore, we're to glorify God
point_summary: Believers are not self-governing authorities; purchased by God at a price, they are owned by Him and therefore obligated to glorify Him in every aspect of their being.

context: for by the grace of God I am what I am
point_summary: Paul's declaration "by the grace of God I am what I am" is cited to distinguish saving grace from serving grace—the God-given ability to fulfill one's calling.

context: I referenced earlier about 1 Corinthians chapter 6, that the Holy Spirit is, that our bodies are the temple of the Holy Ghost, which is in you, which you have of God.

context: We could suffer loss. We could receive reward, according to 1 Corinthians.

context: Know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which you have of God, and you're not your own
point_summary: Because the believer's body is the temple of the Holy Spirit and was purchased at the price of Christ's blood, the saved person is no longer self-owner and is therefore obligated to glorify God through a surrendered life.

context: for you are bought with a price. Therefore, because you've been bought with a price, we are to glorify God
point_summary: Purchased by Christ's blood, believers are no longer their own, and this ownership is the compelling reason to glorify God through a surrendered life.

context: therefore glorify God in your body and your spirit which are God's
point_summary: Being purchased by Christ's blood means believers are no longer self-owners, and that transferred ownership is the direct ground for living a surrendered, God-glorifying life.

context: I've been bought with a price
point_summary: Being purchased by Christ's blood means the believer is no longer self-owned, and that transferred ownership is the very ground for living a surrendered, God-glorifying life.

context: Moreover it is required in stewards, that a man be found faithful.
point_summary: Faithful stewardship of the gospel is not optional but required, as Paul's own words to Corinth—"it is required in stewards that a man be found faithful"—frame his desire to fulfill the ministry entrusted to him.

context: God has chosen that He would use preaching, the foolishness of preaching
point_summary: God's chosen method of making Christ known is preaching, a point grounded in 1 Corinthians 1:21 and reinforced by Paul's own stated commission to declare the gospel in Spirit-empowered proclamation.

context: for Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel, not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect.
point_summary: Paul's divine commission was specifically to preach the gospel, not in rhetorical wisdom, lest the cross lose its power—establishing preaching as Spirit-dependent proclamation rather than human eloquence.

context: for after that, in the wisdom of God, the world by wisdom knew not God, it pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe.
point_summary: God's chosen method for saving those who believe is the proclamation of Christ crucified — dismissed as foolishness by the world yet the very power and wisdom of God.

context: Paul says in 1 Corinthians 4, in verse number 14, I write not these things to shame you, but as my beloved sons, I warn you.
point_summary: Warnings from a minister of the gospel are acts of love, not shame — Paul's own words in 1 Corinthians 4:14 establishing that faithful warning flows from fatherly affection for those being warned.

context: further on in 1 Corinthians, in chapter number 10, Paul will use the illustration of some Old Testament examples
point_summary: Paul's use of Old Testament examples in 1 Corinthians 10 demonstrates that behaviors displeasing to God then remain displeasing to God now, making those warnings perpetually applicable.

context: He says, for I am the least of the apostles, that I'm not meant to be called an apostle because I persecuted the church of God.
point_summary: Paul's self-description as the least of the apostles who nonetheless labored abundantly grounds the sermon's point that divine grace, not personal merit, is the sole enabling power for ministry.

context: But by the grace of God, I am what I am. And His grace which was bestowed upon me was not in vain, but I labored more abundantly than they all. Yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "by the grace of God I am what I am" is read as the ground of his ministerial enablement—divine grace, not personal merit, empowering his labor.

context: Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal.
point_summary: Charity's defining quality in 1 Corinthians 13:4—"suffereth long"—uses the same Greek word translated "long-suffering" in Galatians 5:22, linking Spirit-produced love directly to patient endurance toward others.

context: Charity in 1 Corinthians 13 and verse number 4 is described this way, it suffereth long.
point_summary: Charity's defining quality in 1 Corinthians 13:4—that it "suffereth long"—uses the same Greek word translated "long-suffering," grounding patient endurance toward others in Spirit-produced love rather than human strength.

context: the Bible describes charity or love in 1 Corinthians 13 as it suffereth long.
point_summary: The Greek word underlying "charity suffereth long" in 1 Corinthians 13:4 is the same word translated "long-suffering," grounding the command to be patient toward all men in the nature of love itself.

context: ...as Paul said, I die daily...
point_summary: Patience toward all men is only achievable through daily self-crucifixion, as Paul's "I die daily" describes the ongoing death to self required to walk in the Spirit.

context: In verse number four of 1 Corinthians 1, notice what Paul says to these believers under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God. He says, I thank my God always on your behalf. Notice this, for the grace of God which is given you by Jesus Christ.
point_summary: God's grace given through Jesus Christ encompasses not only saving grace but also serving grace — the divine enablement by which believers are equipped to serve the Lord.

context: the grace of God which is given you by Jesus Christ
point_summary: Grace given by Jesus Christ encompasses not only saving grace but also serving grace — the God-given ability and equipping to serve him.

context: in everything, verse number five, ye are enriched by him in all utterance and in all knowledge

context: even as the testimony of Christ was confirmed in you
point_summary: God's grace given through Jesus Christ encompasses not only saving grace but also serving grace — the divine enablement by which believers are equipped and gifted to serve Him.

context: Notice verse seven, so that you come behind in no gift, waiting for the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ

context: And these things brethren I have in a figure transferred to myself and to Apollos for your sakes that you might learn in us not to think of men above that which is written, that no one of you be puffed up for one against another.
point_summary: Corinthian pride over spiritual gifts is rebuked by Paul's reminder that no believer possesses anything not received from God, making boasting against one another a contradiction of grace.

context: And these things brethren I have in a figure transferred to myself and to Apollos for your sakes that you might learn in us not to think of men above that which is written, that no one of you be puffed up for one against another.
point_summary: Every spiritual gift is received, not achieved — Paul's rhetorical question "what hast thou that thou didst not receive?" dismantles the pride driving Corinth's divisions by tracing all gifting back to God alone.

context: And notice in verse 7 he says, for who maketh thee to differ from another? ... and what hast thou that thou didst not receive?
point_summary: Every spiritual gift and capacity for service is entirely God's provision, not personal achievement — making pride over one's gifts a failure to recognize that nothing was self-generated.

context: In 1 Corinthians chapter 12, notice as he continues to help this group of believers in Corinth to understand the subject matter of spiritual gifts... Notice in 1 Corinthians 12, now, verse number one, concerning spiritual gifts, brethren, I would not have you ignorant.

context: Wherefore, I give you to understand that no man speaking by the Spirit of God calleth Jesus a curse, and that no man can say that Jesus is the Lord, but by the Holy Ghost.

context: Verse number five, and there are differences of administrations, but the same Lord.

context: And there are diversities of operations, but it is the same God which worketh all in all.

context: But the manifestation of the Spirit is given to every man to profit with all.

context: we'll reference one said problem that's in 1 Corinthians 5 this morning in this text of scripture in 2 Corinthians 2.
