context: In 1 Corinthians chapter number five, Paul says
point_summary: The gross moral sin addressed in 1 Corinthians 5 through church discipline and rebuke is the same case Paul revisits in 2 Corinthians 2, where repentance now obligates the church to forgive.

context: I am the least of the apostles that am not meet to be called an apostle, because I persecuted the church of God.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that he labored "by the grace of God" illustrates that divine grace functions not only as saving grace but as enabling, serving grace that empowers faithful ministry.

context: But by the grace of God, I am what I am. And notice, and his grace, which was bestowed upon me, was not in vain. But I labored more abundantly than they all, yet not I,
point_summary: Paul's declaration that he labored "yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me" illustrates that God's grace functions not only as saving grace but as daily enabling grace for faithful service.

context: I was speaking there from the book of 1 Corinthians, chapter number 1
point_summary: During times of conflict, reviewing what we know to be true is identified as a necessary response—drawn from a prior sermon on divisions and contentions in 1 Corinthians 1.

reference_text: Thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through the Lord Jesus Christ
context: Thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Victory over defeat is not merely a future hope but a present calling, grounded in the promise that God gives believers the victory through the Lord Jesus Christ.

context: but thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Victory over the defeated enemy is already promised to believers, making a life of defeat inexcusable and steadfast, abounding labor in the Lord the only fitting response.

context: we're to be steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord, knowing that our labor, well, our labor's not in vain
point_summary: Because God gives believers victory through Christ, steadfast and unmovable labor in his work is never in vain.

context: For though I preach the Gospel, I have nothing to glory of, for necessity is laid upon me. Yea, woe is unto me if I preach not the Gospel.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that "woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel" illustrates the compulsory, debt-like weight of gospel responsibility that drove him from city to city.

context: for while one saith, I am of Paul, and another, I am of Apollos, are you not carnal?
point_summary: Partisan loyalty to individual ministers — "I am of Paul" or "I am of Apollos" — is rebuked as carnality, the very opposite of the cooperative spirit the four men in Mark 2 modeled.

context: The Bible says, who then is Paul and who is Apollos? You notice these words, but ministers.
point_summary: Faithful gospel work requires cooperation among ministers—planting, watering—while recognizing that God alone gives the increase, with no single laborer claiming the glory.

context: I have planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase.
point_summary: Faithful gospel work involves distinct roles — planting and watering — but both laborers are one, and only God gives the increase, making human cooperation subordinate to divine sovereignty.

context: So then neither is he that planteth anything, neither he that watereth, but God that giveth the increase.
point_summary: Human effort in planting and watering the gospel is necessary but secondary—God alone gives the increase, making all who labor together one in a shared, cooperative work.

context: Now, he that planteth and he that watereth, notice what it says, are one. They are one. And every man shall receive his own reward according to his own labor.
point_summary: Each believer's gospel labor—whether planting or watering—is equally valued before God, who rewards every man according to his own work while giving the increase himself.

context: for we are laborers together with God. Ye are God's husbandry, ye are God's building.
point_summary: Believers engaged in gospel work—some planting, some watering—are united as laborers together with God, who alone gives the increase.

context: the analogy of running a race is in clear view
point_summary: Running the Christian life is a race requiring intentional effort, as Paul's analogy in 1 Corinthians 9:24 is cited to reinforce Hebrews 12's call for every believer to run.

context: and this I do for the gospel's sake, that I might be partaker thereof with you.
point_summary: Paul's motivation for gospel partnership—running the race for the gospel's sake to become a fellow partaker of it—is contrasted with athletes who endure discipline only for a corruptible crown.

context: know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize. So run that ye may obtain.
point_summary: Paul's use of the race analogy contrasts the athlete's pursuit of a perishable crown with the believer's pursuit of an incorruptible one, urging purposeful, disciplined running to obtain the prize.

context: and every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things. Now, they do it, those that run those temporal races, they do it to obtain, look at what he says, a corruptible crown.
point_summary: The Christian's race, unlike the athlete's pursuit of a perishable crown, is run for an incorruptible one, making the discipline and self-control required all the more worthy of effort.

context: I therefore so run, not as uncertainly, so fight I, not as one that beateth the air,
point_summary: Paul's purposeful, disciplined running—contrasted with aimless effort—illustrates that the Christian race demands intentional, self-controlled pursuit of an incorruptible crown rather than a perishable one.

context: These that are mentioned in 1 Corinthians chapter number 9, when they would go to compete in those games, they would train, and they would train diligently, and they would prepare, and they would suffer and do various things to themselves, self-infliction, so that they could run to obtain, as Paul says, a corruptible crown, something that would fade away.
point_summary: Athletic competitors in Paul's day trained diligently and endured self-denial to win a perishable crown, illustrating the disciplined preparation required for the Christian life's spiritual race.

context: what Paul says at the end of the book of 1 Corinthians, really the 15th chapter
point_summary: Steadfastness and tireless labor in the Lord's work are not futile, grounding the call to finish one's race with joy and an incorruptible crown.

context: therefore, my beloved brethren, be steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord. For you know that your work is not in vain
point_summary: Steadfast, unmovable labor in the Lord is not in vain, and that assurance grounds the call to finish the race of faith with joy.

context: Paul will reference some of those types of things like what we see in verse number 6 in 1 Corinthians chapter number 10.

context: First Corinthians 11 says, let he that thinketh he stand, take heed lest he fall, amen? I think it's verse 12 in First Corinthians 11, verse 12.
point_summary: Self-confidence about one's spiritual standing is itself a vulnerability, and the warning "let him that thinketh he stand take heed lest he fall" reinforces the call to guard against a heart that deceives.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter 11. I want you to start in verse 17. I'll lay some foundation here.
point_summary: Paul's rebuke in 1 Corinthians 11:17 is introduced as a passage about coming together for worse rather than better, grounded in the divisions and self-serving behavior that corrupt genuine church fellowship.

context: For first of all, when you come together in the church, I hear that there are divisions among you, and I partly believe it.
point_summary: Divisions in the Corinthian church serve as the backdrop for Paul's Lord's Supper instruction, showing that unworthy participation is inseparable from the problem of broken unity among believers.

context: For there must be also heresies among you, that they which are proved may be made manifest among you.
point_summary: Divisions and heresies within the church serve a revealing purpose: they expose and make manifest those who are genuinely approved among God's people.

context: When you come together, therefore, at one place, this is not to eat the Lord's Supper.
point_summary: Gathering for the Lord's Supper while harboring divisions and selfish disorder means the assembly comes together "not for the better, but for the worse," making the observance a condemnation rather than a communion.

context: For in eating every one taketh before the other his own supper, and one is hungry and another is drunken.
point_summary: Divisions and selfish excess at the Corinthian common meal had so corrupted the gathering that it no longer functioned as the Lord's Supper but as an occasion for shame and disorder.

context: What, have you not houses to eat and drink in, or despise ye the church of God, and shame them that have not? What shall I say unto you? Shall I praise you in this? I praise you not.
point_summary: Divisions, selfish indulgence, and contempt for the poor at Corinth's common meal exposed a failure to discern the Lord's Supper as a sacred memorial of Christ's death.

context: 'For I received of the Lord that which also I delivered unto you, that the Lord Jesus, the same night in which he was betrayed, took bread.
point_summary: Paul's reception of the Lord's Supper tradition directly from the Lord grounds the ordinance's authority and frames its proper observance as a memorial of Christ's broken body and shed blood.

context: And when he had given thanks, he broke it, and he said, Take, eat. This is my body, which is broken for you. This do in remembrance of me,'
point_summary: The bread broken and given "in remembrance" identifies the Lord's Supper as a memorial—a symbol and emblem of Christ's death, not a re-presentation of it.

context: 'After the same manner also he took the cup, and after he had supped and sang, This cup is the new testament of my blood. This do ye as often as ye drink it in remembrance of me.
point_summary: The cup of the new covenant is to be received as a deliberate memorial of Christ's death, not carelessly, lest the participant incur judgment for dishonoring the Lord's body.

context: For as often as ye eat this bread and drink this cup, ye do show, demonstrate the Lord's death until he come.'
point_summary: The Lord's Supper is a proclamation—a visible demonstration of Christ's death that continues until his return.

context: Verse 27, 'Wherefore, whose service shall eat this bread and drink of this cup unworthily?'
point_summary: Partaking of the Lord's Supper "unworthily" means approaching it carelessly or irreverently, since none are inherently worthy of Christ's death and blood, making self-examination before communion essential.

context: 'But let a man examine himself,' you see that, 'and so let him eat of that bread and drink of that cup.'
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Supper is not optional formality but a serious command, since careless participation brings divine judgment rather than blessing.

context: For he that eateth and drinketh unworthily or carelessly eateth and drinketh damnation or judgment to himself, not discerning or honoring the Lord's body.
point_summary: Eating and drinking the Lord's Supper carelessly or irreverently brings divine judgment, making self-examination before the table a serious and necessary act of obedience.

context: For this cause many are weak and sickly among you, many sleep.
point_summary: Careless or irreverent participation in the Lord's Supper brings divine judgment expressed as physical weakness, sickness, or death, which God uses as chastisement to keep believers from final condemnation.

context: For if we would judge ourselves, we should not be judged.
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Supper is the God-ordained means by which believers avoid divine chastisement, since judging ourselves spares us from being judged by God.

context: But when we are judged, we're chastened to the Lord.
point_summary: Divine chastening of believers who partake unworthily is evidence of salvation, not condemnation—proof that God disciplines His own so they will not be condemned with the world.

context: But 1 Corinthians 15 and verse number 58 reminds us that God has promised to us
point_summary: God's promise that Christian labor is not in vain grounds the call to remain steadfast and abounding in His work, even when progress feels uncertain.

context: therefore, my beloved brethren, be steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord. Why? For as much as you know that your labor is not in vain in the Lord.
point_summary: God's promise that labor is not in vain in the Lord frees believers from measuring faithfulness by visible fruit, since the work itself—not the results—is their duty.

context: some plant, some water, but God gives the increase.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: our labor is not in vain in the Lord
point_summary: Faithful labor for God is never wasted, even when results are invisible, because God himself is at work and guarantees the increase.

context: The Bible tells us in 1st Corinthians chapter number 15 that this mortal shall put on immortality, that this corruption shall put on incorruption
point_summary: The promise that "this mortal shall put on immortality" grounds the believer's assured hope of receiving a glorified body at Christ's return.

context: The Bible tells us in 1 Corinthians 11 we observe the Lord's Supper, but let a man examine himself.
point_summary: The Lord's Supper command to self-examination in 1 Corinthians 11 is invoked as a biblical pattern for the kind of heart-searching Peter underwent when Jesus questioned his love.

reference_text: let him that thinketh he stand take heed lest he fall
context: Because I believe tonight that every single one of us, let him that thinketh he stand take heed lest he fall, that's Bible, right, that's what it says.
point_summary: Self-confidence about one's standing before God is warned against, as the danger of a divided or fallen heart threatens even those who believe they stand firm.

context: Paul says it this way in 1 Corinthians 6 and verses 19 and 20. What? Know ye not?
point_summary: Redemption by purchase carries an ownership claim: because believers have been bought with a price, glorifying God in body and spirit is the only fitting response to Christ's preeminence.

context: Paul says it this way in 1 Corinthians 6 and verses 19 and 20. What? Know ye not?
point_summary: Redemption by purchase carries an ownership claim: because believers have been bought with a price, glorifying God in body and spirit is the only fitting response to Christ's preeminence.

context: That you've been bought with a price. Therefore, the natural conclusion of being bought with a price is that you're to glorify God in your body and your spirit, which are His.
point_summary: Being bought with a price means Christ owns the believer entirely, making the glorification of God in body and spirit the only fitting response to His preeminence.

context: And here in 1 Corinthians chapter number 1 and verse number 9, notice what Paul says. God is faithful. By whom you were called unto the fellowship of His Son Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: God's faithfulness is not a developing quality but a constant, defining attribute—He is always trustworthy and dependable, as Paul's declaration "God is faithful" in this verse confirms.

context: But by the grace of God, I am what I am. And His grace which was bestowed upon me was not in vain, but I labored more abundantly than they all. Yet not I, but the grace of God which was with me.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "by the grace of God I am what I am" is read as evidence that divine grace is not only saving but enabling—a "serving grace" that empowers and is validated by fruitful labor.

context: in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye, at the last trump, for the trumpet shall sound, and the dead shall be raised incorruptible, for we shall be changed.
point_summary: At Christ's appearing, the believer's corruptible body will put on incorruption and the mortal will put on immortality, grounding the certainty that "we shall be like Him."

context: I'm so thankful of the truth of 1 Corinthians chapter number 11, and what a blessing. I'm so thankful that God gave us the ordinance of the Lord's Supper so that we could remember His death until He come.
point_summary: Revival readiness requires the self-examination commanded in 1 Corinthians 11:28, applied here beyond the Lord's Supper context to the broader practice of scrutinizing one's life before God.

context: But in verse number 28 of 1 Corinthians 11, the Bible says, but let a man examine himself.
point_summary: Self-examination before the Lord's Supper is presented as a necessary, though uncomfortable, discipline that aligns a believer's self-assessment with God's actual view of their life.

context: ...and we consider it in light of Ephesians 5 and 1 Corinthians 11...
point_summary: Self-examination, commanded in 1 Corinthians 11:28, is presented as the necessary prerequisite to honestly inviting God's deeper searching, as the psalmist does in Psalm 139.

context: I am what I am by the grace of God.
point_summary: Genuine self-examination through contemplation humbles believers, reminding them—as Paul confessed—that whatever they are and have accomplished is solely by the grace of God.

reference_text: but thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ
context: ...but thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ...
point_summary: Victory over every opposing force is grounded in God's gift of victory through Jesus Christ, as Paul's declaration in 1 Corinthians 15 assures the church that it is more than a conqueror.

context: we're told in the first chapter that there in the church in Corinth that there are divisions and there are contentions among them
point_summary: Divisions and contentions within the Corinthian church serve as a negative contrast to brotherly love, illustrating what a local church ought not to be.

context: Though I speak with the tongues of men and of angels, and have not charity, I am become a sounding brass or a tinkling cymbal.
point_summary: Without love, even the most impressive spiritual gifts and sacrifices amount to nothing — love is the indispensable quality that gives all Christian action its worth.

context: And though I have the gift of prophecy and understand all mysteries and all knowledge, and though I have all faith, so that I could remove mountains and have not charity, what does it say? I am nothing.
point_summary: Without love, even the most extraordinary spiritual gifts and sacrificial deeds amount to nothing — a point drawn directly from 1 Corinthians 13:2's declaration "I am nothing."

context: Verse three, And though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor, and though I give my body to be purned, and have not charity, it profiteth me nothing.
point_summary: Even the most sacrificial outward acts — giving all one's goods or surrendering one's body — profit nothing if they are not motivated by genuine, sincere charity.

context: Notice how charity is described, it suffereth long, is kind, charity envieth not, charity vaunteth not itself, is not puffed up,
point_summary: Genuine love, defined by God in 1 Corinthians 13, is indispensable above every spiritual gift or sacrifice, and its defining characteristic is that it never fails.

context: doth not behave itself unseemly, seeketh not her own, is not easily provoked, thinketh no evil,
point_summary: Love that is genuine bears all things, believes all things, hopes all things, endures all things, and never fails — qualities cited here to contrast sincere Christian love with hypocritical, self-serving motives.

context: rejoiceth not in iniquity, but rejoiceth in the truth,
point_summary: Genuine love, defined by 1 Corinthians 13, rejoices not in iniquity but in truth—contrasting the sincere, unmotivated charity God requires with the world's counterfeit, self-serving definitions of love.

context: beareth all things, believeth all things, hopeth all things, endureth all things,
point_summary: Charity's comprehensive, self-giving character—suffering long, bearing all things, and never failing—establishes the standard against which all human expressions of love must be measured.

context: and then notice the first phrase of verse number eight, charity never faileth.
point_summary: Charity's permanence and supremacy — it never fails and is the greatest of the three abiding virtues — establish it as the standard against which all Christian love must be measured.

context: It says at the end of the chapter in verse 13, And now abideth faith, hope, charity. These three. But the greatest of these is charity.
point_summary: Among faith, hope, and charity, charity is declared the greatest, underscoring that love holds supreme rank above every other Christian virtue.

context: Paul wrote to the church in Corinth under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God, 1 Corinthians 13. We know it as being what? 1 Corinthians 13 is? Come on, you guys asleep or what? The love chapter.
point_summary: Without love, even the most impressive spiritual gifts and deeds amount to nothing—a warning Paul directed specifically to a deeply troubled, problem-ridden church.

context: Think about the context of who's being written to in 1 Corinthians 13. A church that had all kinds of problems, right?
point_summary: Paul's placement of 1 Corinthians 13 within a church torn by carnal divisions and spiritual-gift rivalry shows that charity is the corrective to pride over God-given gifts.

context: 1 Corinthians 6 speaks about our bodies being the temple of the Holy Ghost, which ye have of God
point_summary: 1 Corinthians 6's teaching that believers' bodies are the temple of the indwelling Holy Spirit grounds the capacity to love others as Christ loved—love made possible by the Spirit within.

context: I've referenced already 1 Corinthians 13. We think about the truth being implemented and the importance of love.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: he's bought with a price, and he's going to glorify God in his body.
point_summary: Bought with a price, the believer's life is not his own, freeing him from self-centeredness and enabling him to press toward finishing his course with joy.

context: Notice what Paul says. I'll close with this in 1 Corinthians 9. In 1 Corinthians 9, the apostle Paul says, in verse 23, and this I do for the gospel's sake, that I might be partaker thereof with you.
point_summary: Sharing in the gospel's blessings requires active, undistracted participation—Paul's stated motive for enduring all things is that he might be a fellow partaker of the gospel alongside others.

context: Know ye not that they which run in a race run all, but one receiveth the prize? So run, notice that ye may obtain.
point_summary: Disciplined, undistracted pursuit of an incorruptible prize—not a corruptible one—is what distinguishes the Christian soldier's race from any earthly athletic competition, demanding the same rigorous self-control Olympic competitors apply to winning.

context: And every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things. Now they do it to obtain a corruptible crown, but we, an incorruptible.
point_summary: Soldiers of Christ must exercise the same single-minded self-discipline as athletes competing for a prize, forsaking all distractions, because our crown—unlike theirs—is incorruptible.

context: And Paul says, I therefore, in verse number 26, so run, not as uncertainly, so fight I, not as one that beateth the air, but I keep under my body and bring it into subjection lest that by any means when I have preached to others, I myself should be a castaway
point_summary: Purposeful, undistracted discipline—running not uncertainly but with a fixed goal—is the mark of one who, like Paul, intends his life and ministry to count rather than be disqualified.

context: know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which you have of God? You're not your own, you're bought with a price
point_summary: The indwelling of the Holy Spirit at salvation—cited from 1 Corinthians 6:19—is presented as the divine enablement that produces genuine love for fellow believers as evidence of new birth.

context: the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God. Why? For they are foolishness unto him, neither can he know them because they are spiritually discerned.
point_summary: The unregenerate person cannot receive or understand spiritual truth because, lacking the indwelling Holy Spirit, the things of God are foolishness to him and beyond his discernment.

context: he recognized that he was bought with a price
point_summary: Paul's full surrender to Christ—recognizing he was bought with a price and living to glorify his Savior—is presented as the foundation of his apostolic fruitfulness and the credibility behind his writing to the Thessalonians.

context: In a moment the twinkling of an eye
point_summary: The rapture described in 1 Thessalonians 4 will occur with the instantaneous speed Paul specifies in 1 Corinthians 15:52 — in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye.

context: he says, I die daily
point_summary: Paul's daily dying — his total surrender to Christ's will — is presented as the motivating disposition that shaped his entire ministry and response to its demands.

context: Mentioned in 1 Corinthians chapter nine. Paul understood God had a purpose for his life
point_summary: Paul's fear of becoming a castaway, referenced from 1 Corinthians 9, grounds his concern that despite great pressures, God's purpose for his life would not be rendered useless.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number 11, verses 24 through 28
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number 11, verses 24 through 28
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number 11, verses 24 through 28
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number 11, verses 24 through 28
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: 1 Corinthians chapter number 11, verses 24 through 28
point_summary: Self-examination under internal pressure—Paul's catalog of sufferings in 1 Corinthians 11:24–28 is cited as evidence that inward, relational pressures weighed on him more heavily than external ones.

context: Christ is the first fruits, 1 Corinthians 15, we will rise again.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection as "firstfruits" in 1 Corinthians 15 grounds the certainty that believers will also rise, making death powerless over the redeemed.

context: Paul says, I'm bought with a price in 1 Corinthians 6.
point_summary: Being bought with a price (1 Cor. 6) grounds the wholehearted walk God demands—because Christ paid it all, half-hearted devotion is inconceivable.

context: But thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Victory over Satan's assault on God's people is grounded in Christ's resurrection triumph, which guarantees that faithful labor for the Lord is never in vain.

context: but now is Christ risen from the dead
point_summary: Christ's resurrection as "firstfruits" guarantees the believer's own future resurrection, grounding the hope that conquered sin, death, and the grave and assuring that God's people will also rise.

context: behold, I show you a mystery. We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed.
point_summary: The resurrection's ultimate victory over death is sealed at the rapture, when the living are transformed and the dead raised incorruptible, fulfilling the mystery that death itself is swallowed up in victory.

context: then shall be brought to pass the saying that is written, death is swallowed up, what does it say? In victory, hallelujah.
point_summary: The fulfillment of 1 Corinthians 15:54's "death is swallowed up in victory" is located entirely in Christ, not in human effort, since the victory is given through the Lord Jesus Christ.

context: Oh, death, where is thy sting? Oh, grave, where is thy victory?
point_summary: Death's taunt — "where is thy sting?" — is answered by the resurrection, which gives believers victory not through their own strength but solely through the Lord Jesus Christ.

context: The sting of death is sin, the strength of sin is the law,
point_summary: Death's sting is identified as sin and its strength as the law, with victory over both belonging solely to God through Jesus Christ—not achieved by human effort.

context: but thanks be to God, which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Victory over death is not self-achieved but is entirely God's gift, given through the Lord Jesus Christ as its sole source.

context: But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Victory belongs to God alone, not human effort — a truth the Gideon narrative illustrates and 1 Corinthians 15:57 confirms by attributing the giving of victory entirely to God through Christ.

context: What did Paul say under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God in 1 Corinthians chapter number 4? He's speaking of stewards, and he says, moreover it is required in stewards that a man be found faithful.
point_summary: Faithfulness is the one universal requirement God places on every steward, regardless of calling or capacity, making it the common ground shared by Moses and every believer.

context: For who maketh thee to differ from another? And then look at this question. And what hast thou that thou didst not receive? Now, if thou didst receive it, why dost thou glory as if thou hadst not received it?
point_summary: Every blessing and ability a person possesses is received from God, not self-generated, making boasting in one's own achievement a failure to recognize the true source of all good things.

context: As every man hath received the gift.
point_summary: Every believer receives a spiritual gift sovereignly assigned by the Holy Spirit, not chosen by the individual, equipping each one for service as God sees fit.

context: Now if Christ be preached that he rose from the dead, how say some among you that there is no resurrection of the dead?
point_summary: Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15 is cited to establish that the resurrection of Christ is the indispensable cornerstone of Christian faith, without which preaching is empty and believers remain in their sins.

context: But if there be no resurrection of the dead, then is Christ not risen:
point_summary: Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15 is cited to establish that without a literal resurrection of the dead, Christ himself is not risen, preaching is empty, and faith collapses entirely.

context: And if Christ be not risen, then is our preaching vain, and your faith is also vain.
point_summary: Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15 is cited to establish that the resurrection is the indispensable cornerstone of Christian faith — without it, preaching is empty and believers remain in their sins.

context: Yea, and we are found false witnesses of God;
point_summary: Paul's argument in 1 Corinthians 15 is cited to establish that if Christ did not rise, apostolic preaching is false witness and faith itself collapses—making the resurrection the indispensable cornerstone of Christian belief.

context: Because we have testified of God that he raised up Christ Whom he raised not up if so be that the dead rise not
point_summary: If Christ is not raised, apostolic witness is false testimony and faith collapses — but the resurrection vindicates both the witnesses and the hope of believers.

context: For if the dead rise not then is not Christ raised
point_summary: If Christ is not raised, faith is empty and sin remains unforgiven — but the resurrection reverses that verdict, grounding the believer's hope, courage, and confidence in a living Savior.

context: and if Christ be not raised your faith is vain Ye are yet in your sins
point_summary: Without the resurrection, faith is empty and sin remains unforgiven — but because Christ is truly risen, believers are not left in their sins and have genuine hope.

context: Then they also which are fallen asleep in Christ are perished
point_summary: If Christ were not raised, believers who have died would be utterly lost — a consequence the sermon cites to underscore why the resurrection is the indispensable foundation of Christian hope.

context: if in this life only we have hope in Christ We're of all men most miserable
point_summary: If Christ were not risen, faith would be vain and believers most miserable — but the resurrection reverses that verdict, grounding present hope and courage in a living Savior.

context: But now is Christ risen from the dead and become the first fruits of them That sleep
point_summary: Christ's resurrection as "firstfruits" guarantees the future resurrection of all who are in him, so believers are not left in their sins nor without hope.

context: for since by man came death by man came also the resurrection of the dead
point_summary: Death entered humanity through one man (Adam), and the resurrection from the dead likewise came through one man (Christ), establishing the resurrection as the direct answer and reversal of Adam's legacy.

context: for as in Adam all die even so in Christ Shall all be made alive
point_summary: Adam's universal death-sentence is answered by Christ's universal offer of resurrection life, grounding the believer's hope in a risen Savior who conquered sin, death, and the grave.

context: But every man in his own order notice verse 23 Christ the firstfruits Afterward they that are Christ at his coming
point_summary: Christ's resurrection as "firstfruits" guarantees the ordered sequence in which believers will also be raised at his coming.

context: moreover brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you which also you have received and wherein ye stand.
point_summary: The gospel Paul declares in 1 Corinthians 15 is presented as evidence — alongside Acts 1's "many infallible proofs" — that Christ's death and resurrection are historically and scripturally verifiable facts.

context: for I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures.
point_summary: Christ's death for sins, as Paul defines it in verse 3, is the first element of the gospel and is grounded in Old Testament Scripture, which the sermon connects to the Passover and substitutionary sacrificial imagery.

context: as defined by the Apostle Paul in 1 Corinthians 15, that which we looked at just a moment ago, that Christ died according to the Scriptures, that he was buried and that he rose again according to the Scriptures
point_summary: Paul's definition of the gospel in 1 Corinthians 15—Christ died, was buried, and rose again according to the Scriptures—is identified as the message believers are commissioned to carry into all the world.

context: As in Adam, all die. We mentioned that in 1 Corinthians chapter number 15, remember?
point_summary: The Adamic solidarity in death ("as in Adam, all die") is set against resurrection life in Christ as the ground for why humanity needed a risen Savior rather than self-earned righteousness.

context: He declared in 1 Corinthians 15 what the gospel is. The gospel is the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Cited as the apostle Paul's own definition of the gospel—the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ—which grounds the church's entrusted responsibility to proclaim it faithfully.

context: For Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel, not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect.
point_summary: Paul's singular commission—to preach the gospel rather than to baptize—is cited as evidence that his motive was to please and glorify God, not to build a personal following amid Corinth's factions.

context: For the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness, but unto us which are saved it is the power of God.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that the cross is foolishness to the perishing but the power of God to the saved is cited to show that his preaching aimed at glorifying God, not human wisdom or sectarian loyalty.

context: That no flesh should glory in His presence, verse 31, that according as it is written, that glorieth let them glory in the Lord.
point_summary: Boasting in the Lord alone, not in human ministers or wisdom, is the proper motive for gospel ministry, as Paul's corrective to Corinthian factionalism makes clear.

context: the Bible says that the natural man received with not the things of the Spirit of God for their foolishness unto him. Neither can he know them. Why? Because they're spiritually discerned. In 1 Corinthians 2 and verse 14.
point_summary: Remembering that the natural man cannot receive spiritual truth—being spiritually blind by nature—should move believers toward compassion and grace in gospel engagement, since they themselves were once in that condition.

context: that our bodies are the temple of the Holy Ghost, which we have of God, which of course, we understand there in 1 Corinthians 6
point_summary: The body as the Spirit's temple (1 Cor. 6:19) establishes the baseline of indwelling, against which the sermon distinguishes a separate, ongoing reality: being filled with the Spirit requires self-surrender.

context: Paul says this in 1 Corinthians 3 and verse number 5, Who then is Paul? And who is Apollos? But ministers by whom ye believed, even as the Lord gave to every man.
point_summary: Missionaries and local believers are fellow laborers in one shared work, each contributing distinct roles—planting, watering—while God alone produces the increase.

context: Paul says this in verse 6 of 1 Corinthians 3, I have planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the increase.
point_summary: Fruitfulness in gospel work belongs entirely to God, who gives the increase, while planters and waterers are merely fellow laborers privileged to participate in what he alone accomplishes.

context: So then neither is he that planteth anything, neither he that watereth, but God that giveth the increase.
point_summary: God alone gives the increase in gospel work, making human planters and waterers nothing in themselves, so that all fruitfulness in missions belongs entirely to him.

context: Now he that planteth and he that watereth are one, and every man shall receive his own reward according to his own labor.
point_summary: Each individual laborer—whether planting or watering—will receive a personal reward according to his own work, yet all serve together under God who alone gives the increase.

context: Notice this in verse number 9 of 1 Corinthians 3, For we are laborers together with God. Ye are God's husbandry, ye are God's building.
point_summary: Believers share in God's redemptive work not as independent agents but as laborers together with God, whose role is to plant and water while God alone gives the increase.

context: Paul was writing to the believers in Corinth he said that he could not write to them as being spiritual but he had to write it to them as being carnal they were babes in Christ he says this you don't need to turn there but in 1st Corinthians chapter 3 in verse number 1 he says and I brethren could not speak and do as unto spiritual but as unto carnal even as unto babes in Christ
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity is not determined by age or years of salvation, as Paul's rebuke of the Corinthians—still carnal "babes in Christ" despite extended teaching—demonstrates the ongoing need to pray for genuine spiritual growth.

context: Notice, you'll recall with me a great truth from 1 Corinthians 15 in verse number 58.
point_summary: Steadfast endurance in circumstances is grounded in the assurance that labor done in the Lord is never in vain, linking 1 Corinthians 15:58's call to be unmovable with Colossians 1:11's patience.

context: First Corinthians chapter number nine, notice what the Bible says in this portion of Scripture. In First Corinthians nine, Paul is writing to the church in Corinth, and he's giving the same truth that he mentioned to those in Rome. He says in verse 11 of First Corinthians nine, if we have sown unto you spiritual things,
point_summary: Elders who labor in spiritual ministry have a biblical claim on material support from the congregation, since sowing spiritual things warrants reaping "carnal" — that is, physical and financial — provision in return.

reference_text: But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ
context: But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Victory over sin's power is grounded in Christ's triumph, not personal willpower, so that believers sin less and less though never reaching sinless perfection in this life.

context: The Apostle Paul made it abundantly clear in chapter number 15 of the book of 1 Corinthians that if he did not raise from the dead, then our faith is vain, our preaching is vain.
point_summary: The resurrection is the indispensable foundation of Christian faith and preaching, such that without it both are rendered utterly vain and hopeless.

context: Well, very simply from 1st Corinthians 15. It's the death barrel and resurrection
point_summary: The gospel, summarized from 1 Corinthians 15 as Christ's death, burial, and resurrection, is the good news the church carries and the power by which God saves.

context: this mortal shall put on immortality. This corruption shall put on incorruption.
point_summary: The bodily resurrection—when "this mortal shall put on immortality"—is cited as the ultimate, radical transformation that awaits believers, surpassing even the change experienced at salvation.

context: the Apostle Paul, as he's speaking to the believers in Corinth in 1 Corinthians 15 and verse number 52, describes that event of the rapture of the church in this manner, in a moment
point_summary: The rapture described in 1 Corinthians 15:52 happens suddenly—"in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye"—distinguishing it from the later appearing of Christ in glory referenced in Colossians 3:4.

context: In a moment, in the twinkling of an eye. And we find that he's discussing in 1 Corinthians 15 the rapture of the church
point_summary: Paul's description of the rapture in 1 Corinthians 15:52 — "in a moment, in the twinkling of an eye" — is cited to distinguish the rapture's sudden, secret character from the later appearing of Christ in glory referenced in Colossians 3:4.

context: this mortal shall put on immortality, that this corruption shall put on incorruption
point_summary: The believer's future glorification—mortal putting on immortality, corruption putting on incorruption—is invoked as the coming reality that frames present Christlike living in the world.

context: Paul says this unto the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God in 1 Corinthians 15, the Gospel is this, the death, burial, and glorious resurrection of our Lord and Savior, Jesus Christ
point_summary: The Gospel that missionaries are sent to proclaim is defined by 1 Corinthians 15 as the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, accomplished according to the Scriptures.

context: That's what the Bible says in 1 Corinthians 15.
point_summary: The Gospel's content—Christ's death, burial, and resurrection according to the Scriptures—is cited from 1 Corinthians 15 as the defining message missionaries are sent to proclaim.

context: We find here, notice over in 1 Corinthians, if you will, and chapter number three,
point_summary: Every believer without exception must appear before the judgment seat of Christ, a certainty Paul addresses in both Corinthian letters as he describes the same eschatological event.

context: according to the grace of God, which is given unto me as a wise master builder, I have laid the foundation, another buildeth thereon, but let every man take heed how he buildeth thereupon.
point_summary: Every believer's work will be fully exposed at the judgment seat of Christ, with 1 Corinthians 3:10–13 cited as Paul's parallel teaching in his first letter confirming the same unavoidable accountability declared in 2 Corinthians 5:10.

context: For other foundation can no man lay, than that is laid, which is Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Jesus Christ as the sole foundation means every believer's work built upon him will be fully exposed and tested at the judgment seat of Christ.

context: Now, if any man build upon this foundation, gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble,
point_summary: The materials a believer builds upon Christ's foundation — gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble — will be tested at the judgment seat, revealing the quality of every man's work.

context: every man's work shall be made manifest.
point_summary: Every believer's works will be fully exposed and tested at the judgment seat of Christ, with 1 Corinthians 3:13 cited as Paul's parallel teaching to 2 Corinthians 5:10 confirming the universality and certainty of that accounting.

context: now if any man built upon this foundation... gold, silver, precious stones
point_summary: At the Judgment Seat of Christ, the "good" and "bad" of 2 Corinthians 5:10 correspond respectively to gold, silver, and precious stones versus wood, hay, and stubble in 1 Corinthians 3:12.

context: the foundation is Jesus Christ
point_summary: Christ Jesus as the sole foundation (1 Cor. 3:11) establishes the basis upon which believers' works—described in verse 12 as gold, silver, and precious stones or wood, hay, and stubble—will be evaluated at the Judgment Seat.

context: The word bad really means, the simple definition of it means worthless, worthless. It's interesting because we find that here in 1 Corinthians 3 and verse number 12, he uses these three words in the way of helping us to understand that which is worthless or that which is bad as wood, hay, and stubble.
point_summary: First Corinthians 3:12's materials — gold, silver, and precious stones contrasted with wood, hay, and stubble — are used to unpack the single word "good" and the word "bad" (meaning worthless) in 2 Corinthians 5:10.

context: The work and the motive we find in 1 Corinthians chapter number 3 it says this of what sort it is at the end of verse number 13, the work and the motive will be tried by fire.
point_summary: At the judgment seat, both the work and the motive behind it will be tried by fire, meaning impure motivation disqualifies even outwardly good deeds from divine approval.

context: the Bible says in verse number 12 about the building, the materials, gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble
point_summary: At the Judgment Seat, the fire that tries each believer's work—gold, silver, precious stones, wood, hay, stubble—is identified with the Word of God, determining what endures and what is consumed.

context: every man's work shall be made manifest, open, made known, revealed. And it says this, for the day shall declare it because it shall be revealed by fire and the fire shall try every man's work of what sort it is
point_summary: At the Judgment Seat, every believer's work will be exposed and tested by fire—identified here with the Word of God—to reveal whether its quality merits reward or loss.

context: in 1 Corinthians chapter 3, how do I respond knowing the reality and the reason should have an impact on every believer?
point_summary: 1 Corinthians 3, paired with 2 Corinthians 5:10–13, grounds the call for believers to evaluate every action and decision in light of their coming appearance before the judgment seat of Christ.

context: What? Know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost, which is in you, which you have of God?
point_summary: Because the body is the temple of the Holy Spirit and believers are bought with a price, God deserves their very best—not leftovers—in every decision and endeavor.

context: And that you're not your own, for you're bought with a price. Therefore, glorify God in your body and your soul, which is His.
point_summary: Bought with the price of Christ's sacrifice, believers owe God their very best—not leftovers—because their bodies belong to Him and He deserves excellence in return.

context: the motives that are spoken of in 1 Corinthians 3 and verse number 13 of what sort it is
point_summary: At the judgment seat, works will be tested to reveal their inward quality and motive—"of what sort it is"—contrasting the external appearance that impresses men with the heart that God alone sees.

context: I'm thankful that Paul declares it this way in 1 Corinthians 1 and verse number nine by simply saying God is faithful.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that "God is faithful" is cited alongside other Scriptures to confirm that Christ's title "faithful witness" in Revelation 1:5 reflects the unchanging faithfulness of God himself.

context: Oh death, where is thy sting? Oh grave, where is thy victory? But thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ. First Corinthians 15, you read the whole chapter.
point_summary: 1 Corinthians 15 is cited as the Bible's most concentrated treatment of the resurrection, underscoring that Christ has conquered death and believers need not fear it.

context: The Bible says in a moment, the twinkling of an eye, which is faster than you can blink, 1 Corinthians 15, that's how he's gonna return at that point.
point_summary: The instantaneous speed of Christ's return at the rapture — "in the twinkling of an eye" — is cited from 1 Corinthians 15 to distinguish that event from his later, visible return to earth.

context: When we go through the process of what we read of in 1 Corinthians 11 that we often read and reference during the time of observing the Lord's Supper, and we understand that it's a table of examination, but let a man examine himself.
point_summary: Self-examination commanded at the Lord's Supper (1 Cor. 11) grounds the believer's humble, intentional invitation for God to search the heart for hidden wickedness.

context: 1 Corinthians 13 deals with the subject matter of charity, and how important that subject matter is.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Take your Bibles. I know I have Acts chapter 20, but I want you to start in the book of 1 Corinthians chapter number nine.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Paul is speaking to these believers in Corinth, and he says, and this I do for the gospel's sake.
point_summary: Paul's gospel-driven motivation — doing all things "for the gospel's sake" — is presented as the foundational philosophy shaping his entire approach to ministry and service.

context: If you were to look back into the chapter, you find verse number 16 about the responsibility of preaching the gospel and that God had called him to this,
point_summary: Paul's divine calling to preach the gospel, referenced in verse 16, is cited as the foundational compulsion shaping his entire philosophy and framework of ministry.

context: so run that ye may obtain
point_summary: Paul's own ministry exemplifies the race-running imperative of 1 Corinthians 9:24—serving the gospel for both divine accountability and shared participation with believers in its reward.

context: and every man that striveth for the mastery is temperate in all things
point_summary: Every man striving for mastery exercises self-control to win a corruptible crown, but Paul applies this athletic discipline to pursue an incorruptible one, running with purpose rather than uncertainty.

context: I therefore so run, not as uncertainly; so fight I, not as one that beateth the air
point_summary: Paul's declaration in v. 26 — "I therefore so run, not as uncertainly" — is presented as the governing posture of his entire ministry: purposeful, disciplined service aimed at obtaining, not running aimlessly.

context: but I keep under my body, and bring it into subjection
point_summary: Paul's self-discipline in keeping his body under subjection is presented as the governing posture behind his ministry—running purposefully so as not to become a castaway, useless to the gospel he preached to others.

context: so run that you may obtain. He shares that in First Corinthians nine.
point_summary: Paul's exhortation to "so run that ye may obtain" in 1 Corinthians 9 is invoked as the animating impulse behind Philippians 3's imagery of pressing toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God.

context: they run to obtain a corruptible, but we an incorruptible.
point_summary: Finishing life's race faithfully, as Paul did, yields an incorruptible crown of righteousness — contrasted with the corruptible crown sought by earthly competitors.

context: so run that ye may obtain.
point_summary: Paul's finishing his race with confidence of an incorruptible crown illustrates the very prize 1 Corinthians 9:24 urges believers to run to obtain.

context: ...so he says, and I believe that he believed this for himself in First Corinthians nine, so run that ye may obtain...
point_summary: Finishing one's God-given ministry course with joy requires the same determined, prize-seeking resolve Paul urged on the Corinthians and applied to his own life.

context: The Bible tells us, moreover, it's required in stewards that a man be found faithful.
point_summary: Faithfulness is the one universal requirement of stewardship — the sermon argues that while gifts and abilities vary, every believer without exception can meet this single non-negotiable demand.

context: First Corinthians 2.14 through chapter three and verse number three.
point_summary: Believers remain in the world after salvation not by accident but by divine purpose, as God's omniscient choice to leave his representatives here rather than immediately transport them to heaven.

context: First Corinthians 2.14 through chapter three and verse number three.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: But the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God. are foolishness unto him, neither can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned.
point_summary: The natural man's inability to receive spiritual truth is rooted in his nature, not merely his will — he cannot know such things because they are spiritually discerned, explaining why a relativistic world rejects absolute truth.

context: But he that is spiritual judgeth all things, yet he himself is judged of no man.
point_summary: The spiritual man, introduced in verse 15, is the first of three distinct categories Paul presents — natural, spiritual, and carnal — which the sermon identifies as the passage's key structural terms.

context: For who hath known the mind of the Lord, that he may instruct him? But we have the mind of Christ.
point_summary: Possessing the mind of Christ, the spiritual person judges all things and stands beyond the judgment of others, in contrast to the natural man who cannot receive spiritual truth.

context: And I, brethren, could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal, even as unto babes in Christ.
point_summary: Paul's introduction of the term "carnal" in 3:1 establishes a third category of persons—distinct from both the natural and the spiritual man—that the sermon sets alongside those two for focused examination.

context: I have fed you with milk, and not with meat, for hitherto ye were not able to bear it, neither yet now are ye able.
point_summary: Milk rather than meat was Paul's necessary diet for the Corinthians because their carnality left them unable to receive deeper spiritual truth.

context: For ye are yet carnal, for whereas there is among you envying, and strife, and divisions, are ye not carnal, and walk as men.
point_summary: Carnality in the believer is evidenced by envying, strife, and divisions — conduct indistinguishable from unregenerate men — marking a third category distinct from both the natural and the spiritual man.

context: We find one of them is given to us in verse 14, the natural man.
point_summary: The natural man's inability to receive the things of the Spirit is introduced here as the first of three categories—natural, spiritual, and carnal—that Paul presents to the Corinthian church.

context: the Bible tells us in verse number 14, as I've read already, that the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God.
point_summary: The "natural man" of verse 14 is identified as the unregenerate, unsaved person who cannot receive spiritual truth because it is spiritually discerned — a condition the sermon frames as spiritual blindness.

context: he mentions in verse 15 about the spiritual, he that is spiritual
point_summary: The term "spiritual" in verse 15 is identified as one of three distinct categories of men—natural, spiritual, and carnal—introduced by Paul as the sermon's organizing framework.

context: he speaks of that in verse number 1 of chapter 3, that I could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, and then this third term, but as unto carnal.
point_summary: Paul's introduction of the term "carnal" in 3:1 completes a three-category taxonomy — natural, spiritual, and carnal — that structures the sermon's entire framework for understanding spiritual discernment.

context: For they are foolishness unto him, neither can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned. Those, as we look in verse number 14, we find that the natural man is referring to the unregenerate man, the unsaved man. That's who's being referred to here in verse number 14 of chapter number 2. And we find that the Bible says that the Spirit of God, if you look here, it says that the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God.
point_summary: Spiritual blindness in the unregenerate is rooted in the absence of the indwelling Spirit, who alone enables a person to receive and understand the things of God.

context: the natural man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of God
point_summary: Spiritual blindness in the unregenerate is explained by the absence of the indwelling Holy Spirit, who alone teaches Scripture, so biblical truth appears as foolishness to the natural man.

context: They are referred to as being the natural man, and they receive not the things of the Spirit of God. Why? Notice what it says. Not only do they receive not the things of the Spirit of God, but it says that they are foolishness unto him. Neither can he know them.
point_summary: Spiritual blindness in the unregenerate explains their moral and political incomprehension, because the natural man neither receives nor can know the things of the Spirit, finding them foolishness.

context: He continues in verse 15 of chapter two. It says, but he that is spiritual judgeth all things.
point_summary: The same Greek word translated "discerned" in verse 14 and "judgeth" in verse 15 links spiritual blindness in the lost to spiritual perception in the believer, who alone can judge all things.

context: For who hath known the mind of the Lord that he may instruct him. And then he says, but we have the mind of Christ.
point_summary: Possessing "the mind of Christ" (v. 16) is not a subjective intuition but is grounded in Scripture itself, making Bible knowledge the only reliable answer to the question of what Christ would do.

context: as we continue into the third chapter of 1 Corinthians, we find that there's a matter of spiritual immaturity
point_summary: Possessing the mind of Christ through Scripture is the only reliable basis for knowing what Jesus would do, making Bible engagement indispensable to genuine spiritual discernment.

context: And I, brethren, could not speak unto you as unto spiritual, but as unto carnal, even as unto babes in Christ.
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity in a genuine believer creates a pastoral limitation, as Paul's calling the Corinthians "brethren" yet addressing them as "babes in Christ" demonstrates that carnality restricts the depth of truth they can receive.

context: I have fed you with milk, and not with meat: for hitherto ye were not able to bear it, neither yet now are ye able.
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity in a believer limits the depth of truth they can receive, so that Paul could only offer milk rather than the meatier instruction they should have been ready for.

context: when I seek to minister to you, I cannot do it as unto spiritual, but I have to do it as unto carnal, as babes in Christ.
point_summary: Spiritual immaturity in the Corinthian church—evidenced by their inability to receive deeper teaching—forced Paul to minister to them as carnal babes rather than as spiritually mature believers.

context: He says in verse number three, for ye are yet carnal. And then he describes what that carnality, how it's going to play out. He says, whereas there is among you envying and strife and divisions, are ye not carnal and walk as men?
point_summary: Envying, strife, and divisions in the Corinthian church are identified as direct symptoms of spiritual immaturity, proving that carnality blocks believers from receiving deeper biblical truth.

context: Paul said, I have some things I wanted to tell you, but I can't because you're not spiritual, you're carnal. Basically that's what he says in 1 Corinthians chapter number 3 verses 1-3.
point_summary: Paul's withholding of deeper teaching from the Corinthians illustrates that spiritual immaturity requires a leader to discern where someone is and meet them there rather than pressing beyond their capacity.

context: ...moreover by them is thy servant warned, and in keeping of them," the Scriptures, "...there is great reward." We looked a couple of weeks ago at the truth in 1 Corinthians 4 and verse number 14 when Paul said, "...I write not these things to shame you, but as my beloved sons, I warn you." I warn you...
point_summary: Paul's warning in 1 Corinthians 4:14 is cited as evidence that his ministry of warning believers was motivated by paternal care, not shame, aimed at protecting them from false teachers.

context: In first Corinthians one in verse 17, he says, for Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel, not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect.
point_summary: Paul's divine commission was specifically to preach the gospel, not to perform rites, because the cross loses its power when obscured by human eloquence or religious ceremony.

context: But Paul said this in the same book of first Corinthians chapter nine, speaking about this,
point_summary: Paul's declaration in 1 Corinthians 9:16 that preaching the gospel is a necessity laid upon him—with woe threatened if he fails—illustrates the personal, inescapable responsibility every believer bears to proclaim Christ.

context: for though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of for necessity is laid upon me, yea, woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel.
point_summary: Paul's declaration that preaching the gospel is a divine necessity laid upon him — "woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel" — is held up as the model of embracing personal responsibility to evangelize.

context: According to what Paul says in 1 Corinthians 15, the gospel very simply defined is the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Paul's definition of the gospel as the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ (1 Cor. 15) anchors the sermon's insistence that personal evangelism must begin with sin and its required payment.

context: Aren't you thankful for that wonderful promise? First Corinthians 15 57 and 58.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Aren't you thankful for that wonderful promise? First Corinthians 15 57 and 58.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: but thanks be to God which giveth us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Victory over the enemy is already granted through Christ, and that settled knowledge of triumph is the very ground on which believers are called to remain steadfast and unmovable in the Lord's work.

context: Therefore, my beloved brethren, be ye steadfast, unmovable, always abounding in the work of the Lord.
point_summary: Steadfastness in the Lord's work is grounded not in visible results but in the certainty of God-given victory, making the believer's labor never in vain.

context: our labor is not in vain in the Lord
point_summary: Steadfast labor for God is never futile, because what believers *know* by revelation—that their work is not in vain in the Lord—must override what circumstances *seem* to suggest.

context: We are bought with a price.
point_summary: Belonging entirely to God through redemptive purchase means Christians must remain continually mindful—not merely aware—that they are owned by Christ, a consciousness the sermon identifies as essential preparation for revival.

context: In 1 Corinthians, if you will, in 1 Corinthians chapter 11, we're familiar with this passage of Scripture as we would look at this passage when we observe the Lord's Supper...
point_summary: Paul's command in 1 Corinthians 11:28 — "let a man examine himself" — grounds the first practical step toward a clear conscience: self-examination is both biblical and necessary for God's people.

context: And he says in verse number 28, Paul, under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit of God of the church at Corinth, he says, but let a man examine himself, and so let him eat of that bread and drink of that cup.
point_summary: Self-examination before communion, grounded in Paul's command to "let a man examine himself," is presented as both biblically mandated and necessary for maintaining a conscience void of offense toward God.

context: Turn over to the book of 1 Corinthians, if you will. I want you to think about this truth. When we think about proving what is acceptable unto the Lord and examining yourselves, these two things kind of combine this morning at this particular crossing point, if you will, of the message. I mentioned already that the self-examination will lead to confession. 1 Corinthians chapter number three.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: know ye not that ye are the temple of God and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you?
point_summary: Paul's warning that defiling the temple of God invites divine destruction grounds the sermon's call to self-examination, since the believer's body, indwelt by the Holy Spirit, is that holy temple.

context: If any man defile the temple of God, him shall God destroy for the temple of God is holy, which temple ye are.
point_summary: Defiling the body as God's temple carries the gravest consequence—divine destruction—because the believer's body is holy, belonging to the indwelling Spirit.

context: He says, know ye not that ye are the temple of God, and that the Spirit of God dwelleth in you?
point_summary: Just as Jesus cleansed the physical temple twice, believers must continually examine themselves to keep their bodies—the temple of the Holy Spirit—from defilement.

context: you could jot down maybe 1 Corinthians 5 in verse 5, we're not going to take the time to go there this morning
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Paul says it this way to the believers in Corinth, he says, what, know ye not that your body is the temple of the Holy Ghost which is in you, which you have of God?
point_summary: The body as the Holy Spirit's temple confirms that every believer is indwelt by the Spirit at the moment of salvation, a permanent residence distinct from the Spirit's filling.

context: You read 1 Corinthians chapter number one. He went there not to have people following him. He went there to preach Christ.
point_summary: Paul's singular aim in going to Corinth, as drawn from 1 Corinthians 1, was not personal acclaim but to preach Christ, rejecting man-centered oratory in favor of gospel proclamation.

context: behold, I show you a mystery. We shall not all sleep, but we shall all be changed in a moment.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of a mystery — that believers will be instantly transformed at the last trumpet, the corruptible putting on incorruption — grounds the sermon's call to maintain an eternal, heavenward perspective amid earthly hardship.

context: The twinkling of an eye, the last trump for the trumpet shall sound and the dead shall be raised incorruptible and we shall be changed.
point_summary: At the resurrection, the mortal body will be instantaneously transformed into an incorruptible, immortal one — a certainty Paul presents with unmistakable joy and confident expectation.

context: for this corruptible must put on incorruption. Notice this mortal must put on immortality.
point_summary: The mortal, corruptible body must be transformed into incorruption and immortality, a resurrection truth Paul writes with joy and confident expectation that heaven is his certain home.

context: Reminded of the truth, not only here at 1 Corinthians chapter 15
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: First Corinthians chapter 1, we're going to look at chapter 1 and 2 before we get into the message this evening
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: As we look in the book of 1 Corinthians in chapter 1, I want you to see what Paul says, and I'm going to read not the entirety of the chapter, but I'm going to read the last
point_summary: Paul's primary calling—and by extension the believer's—was to preach the gospel, not to get sidetracked by secondary matters, lest the cross of Christ be emptied of its effect.

context: lest any man should say that I had baptized in my own name
point_summary: Paul's clarifying remark about baptism in 1 Corinthians 1:15 serves as the entry point into his declaration that Christ sent him not to baptize but to preach the gospel—the sermon's central concern about prioritizing gospel proclamation in every conversation.

context: I baptized also the household of Stephanas, besides I know not whether I baptized any other
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel, not with wisdom of words, lest the cross of Christ should be made of none effect
point_summary: Paul's primary calling was not baptism but gospel proclamation, and that proclamation must not rely on rhetorical wisdom lest the cross be emptied of its power.

context: for the preaching of the cross is to them that perish foolishness, but unto us which are saved, it is the power of God
point_summary: The gospel message that saves believers registers as foolishness to the lost, yet it remains the power of God—a contrast that cannot be overcome by clever or persuasive words.

context: for it is written, I will destroy the wisdom of the wise and will bring to nothing the understanding of the prudent.
point_summary: God's destruction of worldly wisdom, declared in 1 Corinthians 1:19, establishes that human intellect cannot produce salvation—only the Holy Spirit working through the preached gospel can.

context: for after that, the wisdom of God, the world by wisdom knew not God. It pleased God by the foolishness of preaching to save them that believe.
point_summary: God deliberately bypasses worldly wisdom so that salvation comes only through the proclaimed gospel—what the world calls foolishness—because it pleased Him to save believers by that means alone.

context: for the Jews require a sign and the Greeks seek after wisdom, but we preach Christ crucified.
point_summary: Despite Jewish demands for signs and Greek pursuit of wisdom, the church's singular calling is to preach Christ crucified—a message that transcends both expectations yet remains the only power and wisdom of God unto salvation.

context: of God is stronger than men. Isn't that encouraging? Verse number 25, praise the Lord.
point_summary: God's apparent foolishness and weakness surpass all human wisdom and strength, a truth the sermon treats as grounds for encouragement in gospel proclamation.

context: Verse 26, for ye see your calling, brethren, how that not many wise men after the flesh, not many mighty, not many noble are called,
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the foolish, weak, and base things of the world to accomplish his purposes so that no human flesh can take glory for what he does.

context: but God hath chosen the foolish things of the world to confound the wise, and God hath chosen the weak things of the world to confound the things which are mighty,
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the foolish and weak things of the world to confound the wise and mighty, so that no human flesh can claim glory for what He accomplishes.

context: and base things of the world, and things which are despised hath God chosen, yea, and things which are not to bring to naught things that are.
point_summary: God deliberately chooses the base, despised, and insignificant things of the world so that no human flesh can claim glory before him.

context: Why? Why did God choose to work this way? Why is it that God has revealed to Paul and now to the church in Corinth the way in which he, God, is going to work? Notice verse 29, that no flesh should glory in his presence,
point_summary: God chooses the weak and base things of the world so that no human flesh can claim glory in his presence — salvation is entirely his work, not man's.

context: that no flesh should glory in his presence. It's all about Christ. And we find that it says, verse 30, but of him are ye in Christ Jesus, who of God has made unto us wisdom and righteousness and sanctification and redemption,
point_summary: God's choice of the weak and base to confound the mighty culminates in verse 30's declaration that Christ himself—not human effort—is the believer's wisdom, righteousness, sanctification, and redemption, so that all glory belongs to the Lord alone.

reference_text: Chapter two, notice in verse number one
context: Chapter two, notice in verse number one, and I, brethren, when I came unto you, came to you, came not with excellency of speech or of wisdom, declaring unto you the testimony of God
point_summary: Paul's deliberate renunciation of rhetorical excellence and human wisdom when preaching Christ crucified illustrates that effective gospel witness rests on God's power, not human eloquence or preparation.

reference_text: for I determined not to know anything among you save Jesus Christ and him crucified
context: for I determined not to know anything among you save Jesus Christ and him crucified
point_summary: Paul's deliberate resolve to know nothing except Christ and him crucified shows that effective gospel witness rests not on rhetorical skill or human wisdom but on the power of God alone.

reference_text: And I was with you in weakness and in fear and in much trembling
context: And I was with you in weakness and in fear and in much trembling
point_summary: Paul's own ministry among the Corinthians—marked by weakness, fear, and trembling rather than eloquent speech—illustrates that God works through humble, yielded instruments so that faith rests on divine power, not human ability.

reference_text: and my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the spirit
context: and my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the spirit
point_summary: Effective gospel proclamation rests not on rhetorical skill or human wisdom but on the Spirit's demonstration of power, so that faith stands in God rather than in men.

context: my speech and my preaching was not with enticing words of man's wisdom, but in demonstration of the spirit and of power.
point_summary: Effective gospel witness depends not on rhetorical skill or preparation alone, but on the Holy Spirit's power, lest faith rest on human argument rather than God.

context: That your faith should not stand in the wisdom of men, but in the power of God.
point_summary: Faith that produces genuine salvation must rest on the power of God rather than human persuasion, meaning dependence on the Holy Spirit surpasses even thorough preparation in effective soul-winning.

context: for though I preach the gospel, I have nothing to glory of. Isn't that a great perspective?
point_summary: Paul's compulsion to preach—"woe is unto me if I preach not the gospel"—reframes evangelism's goal away from personal glory or winning arguments toward gaining souls, as his servant-to-all posture in verses 19–23 illustrates.

context: For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward, but if against my will a dispensation of the gospel is committed unto me
point_summary: Paul's willingness to preach the gospel without personal glory or charge illustrates that the evangelist's reward lies not in recognition but in making the gospel freely accessible to gain souls.

context: Verily, that when I preach the gospel, I may make the gospel of Christ without charge, that I abuse not my power in the gospel.
point_summary: Paul's reward in preaching the gospel is the gospel itself offered free of charge, illustrating a self-emptying motivation that removes personal ambition as any obstacle to evangelism.

context: For though I be free from all men, yet have I made myself servant unto all, that I might gain them more.
point_summary: Paul's willingness to become a servant to all (v. 19) is the foundation of his contextual flexibility in evangelism—not a license for doctrinal compromise, but a commitment to removing personal obstacles from the gospel's path.

context: unto the Jews I became as a Jew, that I might gain the Jews. To them that are under the law is under the law, that I might gain them that are under the law.
point_summary: Paul's adaptability to different audiences ("unto the Jews I became as a Jew") is not a license for doctrinal compromise but a commitment to removing personal obstacles that would hinder others from receiving the gospel.

context: To them that are without law is without law.
point_summary: Paul's adaptability in 1 Corinthians 9:19–22 is not a license for doctrinal compromise but a commitment to removing personal obstacles so the gospel itself remains the only stumbling block.

context: I am made all things to all men, that I might by all means save some.
point_summary: Paul's declaration to become "all things to all men" is clarified as a commitment to remove personal cultural barriers from the gospel's path, not a license for doctrinal compromise or ecumenism.

context: remember what Paul said there in 1 Corinthians 1.17, for Christ sent me not to baptize, but to preach the gospel.
point_summary: Paul's commission—sent not to baptize but to preach the gospel—grounds the call to move every conversation purposefully toward the truth of Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.
